Profile Report.Profile Summary: Stephanie, your responses indicate that you have a strong sense of loyalty, duty, and fairness to people. You use a great deal of persuasion when communicating with fellow workers, and you focus on completing activities in the correct and prescribed manner. Rushing through tasks does not appeal to you. Detail work, especially as it relates to helping or working with people, is your express capability. You communicate in a friendly, warm, caring, thoughtful and precise manner.

High Trait.Key Points Attributed to Your High Trait:
Dedicated to maintaining highest quality and efficiency
Able to manage a multitude of details
Exceptionally systematic and disciplined
Inspires and challenges others to excellence
Committed to high ethical standards
High Trait.Candidate Description:
Stephanie, in addition to the qualities above which generate the power behind your work, you are a conscientious and skillful professional, who is dutifully committed to achieving exacting standards while meeting pressure and deadlines. You thrive in an environment that allows you to develop or use systems, which you feel are essential to the smooth operation of a program or project.

You show sensitivity and responsiveness when it comes to motivating praise and demotivating criticism. Because of your strong organizational skills, you can be relied upon to implement company policy and to support colleagues, as long as the principles and actions involved are fair to all concerned.

Since you are able to focus on specific tasks while keeping an overall project goal in mind, you are skilled in setting priorities and like to provide your people with exact direction as to when and how a project is to be done. You may prefer to explore all options and avenues when solving problems or when asked to make changes.
High Trait.Sensitive Areas: An undependable, rapidly changing environment wherein systems are being constantly altered or disregarded; and misdirected criticism, which causes a marked decline in the recipient's attitude and productivity.
High Trait.Potential Reactions: When an issue is dealt with in an improper manner or when criticism is unfair, you may react with intense disapproval; and you may be compelled to counteract by overpowering the opposition with appropriate facts or rules.

Other Traits.Intro: Your other three traits, Dominance, Extroversion, and Patience, and their location in your profile are listed in the Summary section, and they are also portrayed on the profile graph. These three traits have a direct effect on your High Trait and how it is interpreted. You will recall that the High Trait accounts for 50 to 70 percent of an individual's behavior; these other three traits account for the remaining 50 to 30 percent.

Interaction1.Descriptive Words: Conscientious | Loyal | A Perfectionist | Sensitive To Judgment | Systems-oriented | Believe In Systems
Interaction1.Summary Paragraph: You perform skillfully and carefully in your field of endeavor and with the people in your environment. You readily follow rules and systems that you respect and find fair. You are reticent in regard to voicing your criticism in public, unless a principle is being disregarded or fairness is being trampled upon.

Interaction2.Descriptive Words: Friendly | Persuasive | Trusting | Outgoing | Idealistic | Empathetic | Enthusiastic
Interaction2.Summary Paragraph: You are intuitive with people and show expertise in dealing with human relations. You prefer to be in a business with a great deal of interaction with people. You should be indoctrinated into the organizational structure of your company and instructed about its rules.

Interaction3.Summary Paragraph: You are inclined to assist people and care for their welfare. Before making a final decision, you may prefer to talk it over with someone else.

Interaction4.Summary Paragraph: You are capable of coping with routine tasks, especially when they involve relating to people. You possess a very dependable, stable and persistent nature.

Motivational Needs.Primary Motivators:
Because your High Trait is Conformity, you will need some of the following factors in your environment in order to be self-motivated and highly productive:
A structured environment that has few sudden or abrupt changes.
The security of basic benefits.
A worked out system and quality products.
Praise for specific accomplishments.
Standard operating procedures and sound actions.
Fairness in dealings with individuals and groups.
Time to do things right.
Motivational Needs.Primary Demotivators:
In contrast, you are likely to be demotivated when:
You are constantly criticized.
The rules are changed without plenty of notice.
There is a lack of procedures, quality and fairness.
There is not a worked out system.
Motivational Needs.Other Motivators:
These motivators are based on the other three traits: because your Dominance trait is lower
There is strong, capable leadership in your environment.
Direction as to what is to be done and when.
A predictable environment that affords a significant amount of protection and peace.
because your Extroversion trait is higher
Opportunities for people-interaction.
Meeting new people and making friends.
The team approach to getting things done.
Opportunities for success and status.
because your Conformity trait is higher
A structured environment.
A worked out system for getting the job done.
Security, training and quality products.

Decision Making Style.Intro:
How you make decisions is influenced not only by your work environment and job responsibilities, but also by your individual personality characteristics.
One common approach to decision-making -- the Rational Decision-Making Style -- relies heavily on observing and analyzing concrete facts. Another common approach -- the Intuitive Decision-Making Style -- relies heavily on a person's intangible "gut feelings." Neither approach should be considered consistently superior to the other. Most people employ a combination of both approaches when they make decisions.
Decision Making Style.Description: Stephanie, your responses indicate that you tend to favor the Intuitive approach to decision-making. You approach decision-making with optimism and enthusiasm, and with little anxiety about the possibility of making mistakes. You tend to be an energetic leader in the decision-making process, and you are likely to generate a variety of new ideas.

Leadership Style.Intro: The range of Leadership Styles is:Authoritative - Persuasive - Mentor - Traditionalist - Adaptable
Leadership Style.Description: You expertly perform your leadership role by managing your people through systems and procedures, and requiring them to follow your organization's rules. You exercise a conservative and watchful style while requiring that projects be completed correctly. You show respect for colleagues' positions and you expect the same in return. You may desire time alone to judge if performance standards and quality controls are being realized by your people. You are good at team building and enjoy a lot of interaction with your subordinates. You pace yourself and your people well in order to reach your goals.

Emotional Intelligence.Description: Stephanie, your responses indicate that your Emotional Intelligence is exceptionally well developed. You tend to understand the emotional makeup of others, and to accurately sense what other people are feeling. Because you find it easy to see the world from another person's perspective, it is likely that you associate with a diverse group of people. You work for reasons beyond money or status. It is likely that you are proficient in both maintaining relationships and networking. You think before speaking, and form carefully considered judgments about people or situations. Furthermore, you are aware of the impact of your emotions on others.

Criteria1.Title: Personal Insight
Criteria1.Intro: The capacity to accurately recognize and understand own emotions as they occur & to base personal self-confidence on an accurate assessment of own abilities.

Criteria2.Title: Self-Discipline
Criteria2.Intro: The capacity to delay gratification when pursuing goals, to speak carefully, & to control negative impulses.

Criteria3.Title: Drive
Criteria3.Intro: The capacity to pursue goals energetically, to relentlessly seek self-improvement, & to persevere despite obstacles or disappointments.

Criteria4.Title: Interpersonal Insight
Criteria4.Intro: The capacity to understand the emotions of others, to adjust own style to interact well with others, & to empathize with alternative perspectives.

Criteria5.Title: Social Agility
Criteria5.Intro: The capacity to be at ease in almost any social situation, to prevent or resolve conflict, to cultivate rapport & build lasting relationships.

Profile Report.Conscientiousness: You recognize the importance of self-discipline, careful planning, and thoroughness in completing tasks. Your above-average ability to completely fulfill specific goals and obligations is complemented by a usually positive sense of motivation.

The Movement of Traits.Description: Stephanie, your responses indicate how you have been moving your traits in order to adjust to the environmental pressures of your job. These adjustments are from the recent past, usually from 4 to 8 weeks prior to taking the survey. Research shows that individuals alter their Environmental/Role Adjustments at least every 3 to 4 months. Please see the Role Adjustment Profile for monitoring this trait movement activity.

Adjustment1.Description: This indicates that you are exerting more control, assertiveness and forcefulness than is natural for you. This might be caused by the pressures in your environment.

Adjustment2.Description: This indicates that one of two changes is happening to you. Either there has been some disappointment (or let down) by a person or persons; or it has become necessary for you to spend more time alone figuring things out and doing more detail work than usual.

Adjustment3.Description: This indicates that you are either wanting or being forced to eliminate details or to delegate some details, in order to place emphasis upon a more general, big-picture perspective. Perhaps you are being more open-minded, taking more risks and not performing in as systematic a way as usual.

Environmental/Role Adjustments (ERAs).How Co-Workers See You:
Stephanie, your survey responses indicate that you are flexing your behaviors in order to perform your job and/or meet the demands of your environment. Because your on-the-job behaviors are not the same as your Primary Profile, your co-workers may view you quite differently than how you view yourself. The following paragraph describes how your co-workers and manager(s) may see you when you are adjusting to the pressures of your job:



Stephanie, your co-workers may observe that you are very patient, steady, cooperative and dependable. You take pride in your work, show self-confidence in your abilities, and are able to improve the processes of routine operations. If you are called upon to improvise with equipment or think of time saving methods, you are capable of performing these functions. Reliable systems that have proved successful in past experiences are your preference. While you are effective at setting work schedule priorities, you are demotivated when there are too many projects in progress at one time.

Stress Level.Intro: Stress is not necessarily all bad; some stress can be healthy. The Stress Level measurement indicates how well you are handling your current environmental demands. The "Good" response indicates that you are coping with your environment effectively, while the other responses indicate varying degrees of difficulty in dealing with it.
Stress Level.Description: Stephanie, your responses indicate that during the above-mentioned period, your stress level was Very Stressed. This indicates that you sometimes felt overwhelmed by the challenges in your environment. You may have desired more support from your colleagues, or for more time to get things done.

Energy Level.Intro:
Energy Level indicates an individual's current degree of vigor, alertness, and responsiveness. Energy Level measures capacity for activity, and Energy can be thought of as an individual's "battery power." Energy might be depleted at an increased rate while operating within a stressful environment or in a managerial role. Food, sleep, and relaxation can recharge one's Energy.
When energy runs out, the following symptoms tend to appear:
Increased susceptibility to accidents and mental errors.
Reduced ability to concentrate.
Reversion from Public Self (Environmental/Role Adjustment) to Basic Self (the Primary Profile).
Energy Level.Description: Stephanie, you currently have a High Energy Level, and can function effectively in a demanding environment. This means you can handle longer days and stress-related situations when necessary to accomplish your goals.

Proactivity.Intro: The Proactive Personality Scale measures how likely an individual is to intentionally and actively create positive change in the work environment. High proactive personalities identify opportunities and act on them; they show initiative, take action, and persevere until they bring about meaningful change. They will often seek ways to improve the work environment as necessary to facilitate effective job performance. Individuals lower on the Proactive Personality Scale will exhibit these behavioral tendencies less regularly.
Proactivity.Description:
Stephanie, your responses indicate that you enjoy working towards the constructive reform of established systems. You operate with your organization's best interests in mind. You do not allow your desire to improve existing systems to detract from routine work that must get done, nor do you overstep the bounds of appropriateness in your pursuit of change. You are more likely to be committed to an organization that values and supports your efforts. You are particularly skilled at appraising situations, identifying problems, and allocating resources to find solutions and drive innovation.
You enjoy trying different things, or doing the same things differently. Because you dread the routine and the ordinary, you are probably a consistent champion of change initiatives. Experimentation that leads to innovation is important to you.

Self-Monitoring.Intro: The Self-Monitoring Scale measures two dimensions of an individual's personality: Behavioral Flexibility and Career Mobility. High Self-Monitors usually find it easy to "flex" their workplace behaviors in order to adapt to specific situations or individuals. High Self-Monitors are comfortable performing a wide variety of roles, and are skilled at impression management and interpersonal communication. High Self-Monitors are more likely to actively seek promotions, and to follow opportunity to different departments, companies or industries. A High Self-Monitor is more likely than others to relocate geographically in pursuit of opportunity. An individual's Self-Monitoring rating does not predict technical ability or performance on job-related tasks.
Self-Monitoring.Description:
Stephanie, your responses indicate that you are committed and principled in your approach to work. You are less responsive than other people to shifting environmental demands. You tend to behave consistently even in stressful situations. It may frustrate you that such consistency is not regularly recognized or rewarded.
You value the freedom to pursue work that is compatible to your own interests. You work best when supervisors provide guidance but refrain from micro-managing.
The behavioral adjustments you are making in response to the demands of your current environment are within the normal range. Any additional demands might begin to negatively impact your effectiveness.

Personal Learning Styles.Intro: The four main learning styles are Activist, Reflector, Theorist, and Pragmatist. The Activist engages enthusiastically in new experiences. The Reflector prefers quietly gathering and pondering information. The Theorist enjoys abstract concepts and logical analysis. The Pragmatist uses a trial-and-error approach to problem solving, and prefers that learning have practical applications.
Personal Learning Styles.Description:
Your primary learning style preference is: Theorist. It is highly probable that you also employ other learning strategies in addition to your primary style, depending on the educational context. The relative strengths of your learning styles are presented in the graph at the bottom of this section. The following profile summary describes your preferred approach to learning.
Stephanie, your responses indicate that you prefer an analytical, conceptual approach to learning. You tend to rely heavily on logical thinking and rational evaluation. You probably tend to be more oriented towards things and symbols, and less towards other people. You learn best in authority-directed, impersonal learning situations that emphasize theory and systematic analysis. You probably do not enjoy unstructured "experiential learning" approaches such as group exercises and simulations.

You are a highly disciplined thinker who sees patterns and connections that other people do not. You take a step-by-step approach to problem solving, and you rely on facts, not feelings, when making decisions. Because abstract conceptualization is a particular strength, you probably find it easy to discern similarities among apparently dissimilar concepts, things, or people. You work hard to minimize uncertainty or ambiguity in your learning environment. Your instructors and fellow learners might regard you as a perfectionist.
Personal Learning Styles.Developmental Suggestions: Stephanie, your responses indicate that you tend to prefer the Activist learning style less than other styles. In order to develop an integrated learning strategy, you should emphasize your critical thinking skills when listening to lectures or presentations. Employ a healthy dose of skepticism even when learning from "experts." Always seek out evidence that supports (or contradicts) the assertions made by others. Contribute to class discussions and ask questions. Volunteer to participate in experiments or demonstrations.
Personal Learning Styles.Sensory Learning Style:
Your responses indicate that you prefer the Auditory Sensory Learning Style. You are probably already skilled at:
Listening attentively to lengthy speeches or lectures.
Understanding and following oral instructions.
Remembering things by repeating them aloud.
In order to develop a more balanced learning style, you may want to try the following:
Creating charts and graphs to make sense of complex information.
Remembering things by picturing them in your head.
Using an outline to follow along during lectures.
Remembering things by writing them down several times.
Conducting experiments that reinforce basic concepts.
Discovering how things work by taking them apart.

You exercise caution when it pertains to new people, systems and procedures. Your patient, non-rushed and conscientious style is qualified by your insistence that tasks are completed correctly. You are very loyal to people whom you respect. Criticism that is launched in your direction may produce a sensitive reaction on your part.

Your dependable and steady nature guides you to make every move count. Since you are easygoing and possess warm friendliness, you do not like to be involved with conflicts and therefore avoid being placed in a position that might cause you to make an enemy. You prefer to be comfortable and peaceful with people. Criticism is a sensitive matter to you. You tend to be a good listener and prefer one-on-one relationships.

High Trait.Description: Pace And Rate Of Motion Trait
High Trait.Key Points Attributed to Your High Trait:
Highly motivated to promote a productive, positive work environment
Perceptive listener
Exceptionally dependable and persistent
Able to balance task needs with employee needs
Effective in balancing warmth with professionalism
High Trait.Candidate Description:
Laura, in addition to the qualities above which generate the power behind your work, you are a collaborative professional, who is inclined to be highly effectual in dealing with routine and in analyzing work flow and communication patterns, in order to maximize the effectiveness of your work team.

You may express your cooperative and supportive disposition by assisting in the successful resolution of conflicts, by promoting harmonious relationships within your organization, and by assessing co-workers' needs carefully.

You thrive in a goal-oriented environment that encourages you to produce at a measured and steady pace, that allows you to direct your energies toward a fixed number of projects to be completed in realistic time frames, and that recognizes your competitive qualities are aimed at realizing success not only for yourself but for your organization.
High Trait.Sensitive Areas: Undue pressure that is applied without justification; and a bombardment of diverse projects that need to be completed at once.
High Trait.Potential Reactions: In order to avoid confrontation, you may appear to accept unfavorable conditions imposed upon you. However, after an appropriate interlude, you will work quietly to restore balance to the situation.

Other Traits.Intro: Your other three traits, Dominance, Extroversion, and Conformity, and their location in your profile are listed in the Summary section, and they are also portrayed on the profile graph. These three traits have a direct effect on your High Trait and how it is interpreted. You will recall that the High Trait accounts for 50 to 70 percent of an individual's behavior; these other three traits account for the remaining 50 to 30 percent.

Interaction1.Descriptive Words: Very Cooperative | Delegate Detail | Roll With The Punches | Avoid Conflict | Thorough | Methodical | Good Memory | Non-judgmental | Easygoing | Casual | Often Artistic
Interaction1.Summary Paragraph: You respond strongly to the condition of your work environment and to the people it contains. You focus on making your every move count and do not waste physical energy. You do not become preoccupied with or delayed by detail work and quality concerns, especially when speed is required. You show skill in attending to activities which require repetitive action.

Interaction2.Summary Paragraph: You function most effectively when you are given guidance and direction, and when you know what is expected and in what time frame. You appreciate strong leadership in your environment.

Interaction3.Descriptive Words: Loyal | Cautious | Conscientious | Sensitive To Criticism | Careful
Interaction3.Summary Paragraph: You like to organize your activities and may make lists in order to facilitate this. You are inclined to be very dedicated to strong leaders whom you respect. You strive to be fair in your dealings with people. You tend to be skeptical of unproven new concepts, until you have had time to prove that they work effectively.

Interaction4.Summary Paragraph: Your warm and friendly nature causes you to be well accepted on a social basis. Since you are usually patient with people, they are likely to consider you an individual who has the makings for a very good friend. You use a subtle selling style to get matters accomplished in the manner that you desire.

Motivational Needs.Primary Motivators:
Because your High Trait is Patience, you will need some of the following factors in your environment in order to be self-motivated and highly productive:
An environment where there is harmony and cooperation.
A minimum of conflicts and a steady, stable structure.
A pace set for you with no sudden or abrupt changes.
Forewarning of changes so that there is sufficient time to adjust.
Encouragement in regard to creativity.
Knowing how your personal efforts contribute to overall goals.
Validation of self-worth.
Motivational Needs.Primary Demotivators:
In contrast, you are likely to be demotivated when:
Constantly pressured at the last minute.
There are too many personality conflicts.
There are too many sudden changes.
Expectations are too high and/or not clear.
Motivational Needs.Other Motivators:
These motivators are based on the other three traits: because your Dominance trait is lower
There is strong, capable leadership in your environment.
Direction as to what is to be done and when.
A predictable environment that affords a significant amount of protection and peace.
because your Extroversion trait is lower
A limited amount of emotional exposure.
Time alone to think creatively and figure things out.
Respected as a person and taken seriously.
Socializing in a circle where people are known one-on-one.
Time to prepare for group presentations.
because your Conformity trait is higher
A structured environment.
A worked out system for getting the job done.
Security, training and quality products.

Decision Making Style.Intro:
How you make decisions is influenced not only by your work environment and job responsibilities, but also by your individual personality characteristics.
One common approach to decision-making -- the Rational Decision-Making Style -- relies heavily on observing and analyzing concrete facts. Another common approach -- the Intuitive Decision-Making Style -- relies heavily on a person's intangible "gut feelings." Neither approach should be considered consistently superior to the other. Most people employ a combination of both approaches when they make decisions.
Decision Making Style.Description: Laura, your responses indicate that you tend to employ a balanced combination of the Rational and Intuitive approaches to decision-making. However, since you place only a moderate emphasis on these two common approaches, it is likely that achieving results and reaching goals are your primary concerns when making decisions.

Leadership Style.Intro: The range of Leadership Styles is:Authoritative - Persuasive - Mentor - Traditionalist - Adaptable
Leadership Style.Description: You perform your leadership role by accepting whatever comes your way, by adjusting to it and then pushing ahead with persistence. You regard timing as very important and this is reflected in your expertise in systematic planning and in working toward your goals. You tend to delegate authority and may be disinclined to delegate details. You require time to be alone and think matters through in order to be sure that projects are proceeding smoothly.

Emotional Intelligence.Description: Laura, your responses indicate that your Emotional Intelligence is exceptionally well developed. You tend to control or redirect any of your negative impulses or moods. You perform your work conscientiously, and delay gratification while pursuing your goals. You perform accurate self-appraisals of your talents and abilities. You are able to attune your own style to the emotional reactions of others. You strive continually for self-improvement. You do not allow negative emotions to interfere with your social interactions.

Criteria1.Title: Personal Insight
Criteria1.Intro: The capacity to accurately recognize and understand own emotions as they occur & to base personal self-confidence on an accurate assessment of own abilities.

Criteria2.Title: Self-Discipline
Criteria2.Intro: The capacity to delay gratification when pursuing goals, to speak carefully, & to control negative impulses.

Criteria3.Title: Drive
Criteria3.Intro: The capacity to pursue goals energetically, to relentlessly seek self-improvement, & to persevere despite obstacles or disappointments.

Criteria4.Title: Interpersonal Insight
Criteria4.Intro: The capacity to understand the emotions of others, to adjust own style to interact well with others, & to empathize with alternative perspectives.

Criteria5.Title: Social Agility
Criteria5.Intro: The capacity to be at ease in almost any social situation, to prevent or resolve conflict, to cultivate rapport & build lasting relationships.

Profile Report.Conscientiousness: You work hard, demonstrate competence and are determined to fulfill your goals. Your efforts are effective, and your methods are usually unassuming and considerate. Your positive motivations, combined with discipline, planning and purposefulness, lead you to fulfill your tasks and obligations with excellence in mind.

The Movement of Traits.Description: Laura, your responses indicate how you have been moving your traits in order to adjust to the environmental pressures of your job. These adjustments are from the recent past, usually from 4 to 8 weeks prior to taking the survey. Research shows that individuals alter their Environmental/Role Adjustments at least every 3 to 4 months. Please see the Role Adjustment Profile for monitoring this trait movement activity.

Adjustment1.Description: This indicates that you are exerting more control, assertiveness and forcefulness than is natural for you. This might be caused by the pressures in your environment.

Adjustment2.Description: This indicates that events are not happening as quickly as you desire. Perhaps deadlines are not being met, and your sense of urgency is increasing with a need to move at a faster pace.

Adjustment3.Description: This indicates that you are either wanting or being forced to eliminate details or to delegate some details, in order to place emphasis upon a more general, big-picture perspective. Perhaps you are being more open-minded, taking more risks and not performing in as systematic a way as usual.

Environmental/Role Adjustments (ERAs).How Co-Workers See You:
Laura, your survey responses indicate that you are flexing your behaviors in order to perform your job and/or meet the demands of your environment. Because your on-the-job behaviors are not the same as your Primary Profile, your co-workers may view you quite differently than how you view yourself. The following paragraph describes how your co-workers and manager(s) may see you when you are adjusting to the pressures of your job:



Laura, your co-workers may observe that you are very patient, steady, cooperative and dependable. You take pride in your work, show self-confidence in your abilities, and are able to improve the processes of routine operations. If you are called upon to improvise with equipment or think of time saving methods, you are capable of performing these functions. Reliable systems that have proved successful in past experiences are your preference. While you are effective at setting work schedule priorities, you are demotivated when there are too many projects in progress at one time.

Stress Level.Intro: Stress is not necessarily all bad; some stress can be healthy. The Stress Level measurement indicates how well you are handling your current environmental demands. The "Good" response indicates that you are coping with your environment effectively, while the other responses indicate varying degrees of difficulty in dealing with it.
Stress Level.Description: Laura, your responses indicate that during the above-mentioned period, your stress level was Very Stressed. This indicates that you sometimes felt overwhelmed by the challenges in your environment. You may have desired more support from your colleagues, or for more time to get things done.

Energy Level.Intro:
Energy Level indicates an individual's current degree of vigor, alertness, and responsiveness. Energy Level measures capacity for activity, and Energy can be thought of as an individual's "battery power." Energy might be depleted at an increased rate while operating within a stressful environment or in a managerial role. Food, sleep, and relaxation can recharge one's Energy.
When energy runs out, the following symptoms tend to appear:
Increased susceptibility to accidents and mental errors.
Reduced ability to concentrate.
Reversion from Public Self (Environmental/Role Adjustment) to Basic Self (the Primary Profile).
Energy Level.Description: Laura, you currently have a Very High Energy Level. You are highly effective in demanding environments. If called upon, you can maintain your effectiveness in the face of longer hours and stress-related situations. You are also able to juggle a multitude of tasks, or can intensely focus on a single task as necessary.

Proactivity.Intro: The Proactive Personality Scale measures how likely an individual is to intentionally and actively create positive change in the work environment. High proactive personalities identify opportunities and act on them; they show initiative, take action, and persevere until they bring about meaningful change. They will often seek ways to improve the work environment as necessary to facilitate effective job performance. Individuals lower on the Proactive Personality Scale will exhibit these behavioral tendencies less regularly.
Proactivity.Description:
Laura, your responses indicate that you are more likely to be involved in activities aimed at improving the lives of other people. Your commitment to helping others may have led you to become a corporate, civic, or community leader. It is very likely that you regularly set effective, change-oriented goals. Efficient scheduling and effective allocation of resources are some of your strongest skills. You fear complacency and continually strive to improve your professional performance.
You have a strong "bias for action." Learning by doing works best for you. It is unlikely than you would abandon an idea before trying to implement it in the real world.

Self-Monitoring.Intro: The Self-Monitoring Scale measures two dimensions of an individual's personality: Behavioral Flexibility and Career Mobility. High Self-Monitors usually find it easy to "flex" their workplace behaviors in order to adapt to specific situations or individuals. High Self-Monitors are comfortable performing a wide variety of roles, and are skilled at impression management and interpersonal communication. High Self-Monitors are more likely to actively seek promotions, and to follow opportunity to different departments, companies or industries. A High Self-Monitor is more likely than others to relocate geographically in pursuit of opportunity. An individual's Self-Monitoring rating does not predict technical ability or performance on job-related tasks.
Self-Monitoring.Description:
Laura, your responses indicate that you value principles over pragmatism. It is important to you that your career is aligned with your personal beliefs. Your behavior at work usually reflects your deeply held values. You value honesty, and you are sometimes surprised by the dishonesty of others.
You value the freedom to pursue work that is compatible to your own interests. You work best when supervisors provide guidance but refrain from micro-managing.
The behavioral adjustments you are making in response to the demands of your current environment are within the normal range. Any additional demands might begin to negatively impact your effectiveness.

Personal Learning Styles.Intro: The four main learning styles are Activist, Reflector, Theorist, and Pragmatist. The Activist engages enthusiastically in new experiences. The Reflector prefers quietly gathering and pondering information. The Theorist enjoys abstract concepts and logical analysis. The Pragmatist uses a trial-and-error approach to problem solving, and prefers that learning have practical applications.
Personal Learning Styles.Description:
Your primary learning style preference is: Reflector. It is highly probable that you also employ other learning strategies in addition to your primary style, depending on the educational context. The relative strengths of your learning styles are presented in the graph at the bottom of this section. The following profile summary describes your preferred approach to learning.
Laura, your responses indicate that you are an exceptionally patient observer of people and things. You have an impartial, careful, and contemplative approach to learning that relies heavily on your intuitive perceptions about human nature. You enjoy quietly pondering ideas and concepts, and you always seek fair solutions. Before forming an opinion, you consider all of the information available, you take your time, and you do not like to be rushed.

You tend to prefer learning situations that allow you to be an objective observer. You prefer that lecturers focus primarily on integrative conceptual models, and not on conflict and controversy. During group discussions, it is likely that you express your opinions diplomatically. In the classroom, you probably tend to keep a relatively low profile. You listen closely to what other people say, and you can probably detect meaning in their vocal tone and body language, as well.
Personal Learning Styles.Developmental Suggestions: Laura, your responses indicate that you tend to prefer the Activist learning style less than other styles. In order to develop an integrated learning strategy, you should emphasize your critical thinking skills when listening to lectures or presentations. Employ a healthy dose of skepticism even when learning from "experts." Always seek out evidence that supports (or contradicts) the assertions made by others. Contribute to class discussions and ask questions. Volunteer to participate in experiments or demonstrations.
Personal Learning Styles.Sensory Learning Style:
Your responses indicate that you prefer the Auditory Sensory Learning Style. You are probably already skilled at:
Listening attentively to lengthy speeches or lectures.
Understanding and following oral instructions.
Remembering things by repeating them aloud.
In order to develop a more balanced learning style, you may want to try the following:
Creating charts and graphs to make sense of complex information.
Remembering things by picturing them in your head.
Using an outline to follow along during lectures.
Remembering things by writing them down several times.
Conducting experiments that reinforce basic concepts.
Discovering how things work by taking them apart.

You may show sensitivity and responsiveness to motivating praise and demotivating criticism.

Because of your strong organizational skills, you can be relied upon to implement company policy and to support colleagues, as long as the principles involved are fair to all concerned.

Decision Making Style.Intro:
How you make decisions is influenced not only by your work environment and job responsibilities, but also by your individual personality characteristics.
One common approach to decision-making -- the Rational Decision-Making Style -- relies heavily on observing and analyzing concrete facts. Another common approach -- the Intuitive Decision-Making Style -- relies heavily on a person's intangible "gut feelings." Neither approach should be considered consistently superior to the other. Most people employ a combination of both approaches when they make decisions.

Energy Level.Intro:
Energy Level indicates an individual's current degree of vigor, alertness, and responsiveness. Energy Level measures capacity for activity, and Energy can be thought of as an individual's "battery power." Energy might be depleted at an increased rate while operating within a stressful environment or in a managerial role. Food, sleep, and relaxation can recharge one's Energy.
When energy runs out, the following symptoms tend to appear:
Increased susceptibility to accidents and mental errors.
Reduced ability to concentrate.
Reversion from Public Self (Environmental/Role Adjustment) to Basic Self (the Primary Profile).
Energy Level.Description: Ryan, you currently have a Very High Energy Level. You are highly effective in demanding environments. Proactivity.Intro: The Proactive Personality Scale measures how likely an individual is to intentionally and actively create positive change in the work environment. High proactive personalities identify opportunities and act on them; they show initiative, take action, and persevere until they bring about meaningful change. They will often seek ways to improve the work environment as necessary to facilitate effective job performance. Individuals lower on the Proactive Personality Scale will exhibit these behavioral tendencies less regularly.
Proactivity.Description:
Ryan, your responses indicate that you are dedicated to changing things, in an intended direction, for the better. You are highly skilled at risk-assessment, but you are not afraid of taking chances, given the appropriate benefit incentive. When faced with change, you excel at instituting new rules for the proper conduct of business. You would prefer to shape the competitive landscape, rather than merely react to the moves of others. By focusing on quality, efficiency, and consistency, you create demand for your organization's products and services.
You are always looking for a better way of doing things. Traditional approaches, even if proven and efficient, do not appeal to you. You enjoy being a pioneer in your field or industry.

Self-Monitoring.Intro: The Self-Monitoring Scale measures two dimensions of an individual's personality: Behavioral Flexibility and Career Mobility. High Self-Monitors usually find it easy to "flex" their workplace behaviors in order to adapt to specific situations or individuals. High Self-Monitors are comfortable performing a wide variety of roles, and are skilled at impression management and interpersonal communication. High Self-Monitors are more likely to actively seek promotions, and to follow opportunity to different departments, companies or industries. A High Self-Monitor is more likely than others to relocate geographically in pursuit of opportunity. An individual's Self-Monitoring rating does not predict technical ability or performance on job-related tasks.
Self-Monitoring.Description:
Ryan, your responses indicate that you are committed and principled in your approach to work. You are less responsive than other people to shifting environmental demands. You tend to behave consistently even in stressful situations. It may frustrate you that such consistency is not regularly recognized or rewarded.
You value the freedom to pursue work that is compatible to your own interests. You work best when supervisors provide guidance but refrain from micro-managing.
You are not making significant behavioral adjustments in response to the demands of your current environment. This may mean that you do not consider your current environment particularly challenging, or that your current position constitutes a "natural fit" for you.

Personal Learning Styles.Intro: The four main learning styles are Activist, Reflector, Theorist, and Pragmatist. The Activist engages enthusiastically in new experiences. The Reflector prefers quietly gathering and pondering information. The Theorist enjoys abstract concepts and logical analysis. The Pragmatist uses a trial-and-error approach to problem solving, and prefers that learning have practical applications.
Personal Learning Styles.Description:
Your primary learning style preference is: Theorist. It is highly probable that you also employ other learning strategies in addition to your primary style, depending on the educational context. The relative strengths of your learning styles are presented in the graph at the bottom of this section. The following profile summary describes your preferred approach to learning.
Ryan, your responses indicate that you prefer an analytical, conceptual approach to learning. You tend to rely heavily on logical thinking and rational evaluation. You probably tend to be more oriented towards things and symbols, and less towards other people. You learn best in authority-directed, impersonal learning situations that emphasize theory and systematic analysis. You probably do not enjoy unstructured "experiential learning" approaches such as group exercises and simulations.

You are a highly disciplined thinker who sees patterns and connections that other people do not. You take a step-by-step approach to problem solving, and you rely on facts, not feelings, when making decisions. Because abstract conceptualization is a particular strength, you probably find it easy to discern similarities among apparently dissimilar concepts, things, or people. You work hard to minimize uncertainty or ambiguity in your learning environment. Your instructors and fellow learners might regard you as a perfectionist.
Personal Learning Styles.Developmental Suggestions: Ryan, your responses indicate that you tend to prefer the Activist learning style less than other styles. In order to develop an integrated learning strategy, you should emphasize your critical thinking skills when listening to lectures or presentations. Employ a healthy dose of skepticism even when learning from "experts." Always seek out evidence that supports (or contradicts) the assertions made by others. Contribute to class discussions and ask questions. Volunteer to participate in experiments or demonstrations.
Personal Learning Styles.Sensory Learning Style:
Your responses indicate that you prefer the Auditory Sensory Learning Style. You are probably already skilled at:
Listening attentively to lengthy speeches or lectures.
Understanding and following oral instructions.
Remembering things by repeating them aloud.
In order to develop a more balanced learning style, you may want to try the following:
Creating charts and graphs to make sense of complex information.
Remembering things by picturing them in your head.
Using an outline to follow along during lectures.
Remembering things by writing them down several times.
Conducting experiments that reinforce basic concepts.
Discovering how things work by taking them apart.

How you make decisions is influenced not only by your work environment and job responsibilities, but also by your individual personality characteristics.
One common approach to decision-making -- the Rational Decision-Making Style -- relies heavily on observing and analyzing concrete facts. Most people employ a combination of both approaches when they make decisions.

Energy Level.Intro:

Energy might be depleted at an increased rate while operating within a stressful environment or in a managerial role. Food, sleep, and relaxation can recharge one's Energy.

Increased susceptibility to accidents and mental errors.
Reduced ability to concentrate.
Reversion from Public Self (Environmental/Role Adjustment) to Basic Self (the Primary Profile).

Proactivity.Intro: The Proactive Personality Scale measures how likely an individual is to intentionally and actively create positive change in the work environment. Proactivity.Description:

You may tend to make job changes more frequently than others in order to experience new challenges, responsibilities, or corporate cultures. You learn best by jumping in and trying new things yourself. You rarely hesitate to take the lead and charge ahead - even in the face of uncertainty.

Self-Monitoring.Intro: The Self-Monitoring Scale measures two dimensions of an individual's personality: Behavioral Flexibility and Career Mobility. High Self-Monitors usually find it easy to "flex" their workplace behaviors in order to adapt to specific situations or individuals. High Self-Monitors are comfortable performing a wide variety of roles, and are skilled at impression management and interpersonal communication. High Self-Monitors are more likely to actively seek promotions, and to follow opportunity to different departments, companies or industries. A High Self-Monitor is more likely than others to relocate geographically in pursuit of opportunity. An individual's Self-Monitoring rating does not predict technical ability or performance on job-related tasks.

Personal Learning Styles.Intro: The four main learning styles are Activist, Reflector, Theorist, and Pragmatist. The Activist engages enthusiastically in new experiences. The Reflector prefers quietly gathering and pondering information. The Theorist enjoys abstract concepts and logical analysis. The Pragmatist uses a trial-and-error approach to problem solving, and prefers that learning have practical applications.
Personal Learning Styles.Description:
Your primary learning style preference is: Theorist. It is highly probable that you also employ other learning strategies in addition to your primary style, depending on the educational context. The relative strengths of your learning styles are presented in the graph at the bottom of this section. The following profile summary describes your preferred approach to learning.
Matt, your responses indicate that you prefer an abstract, conceptual approach to learning. You tend to make good use of logical thinking and rational evaluation. You probably tend to be more oriented towards symbolism, and less towards concrete facts. You learn best from experts, and in learning situations that emphasize theories and systems. You probably do not enjoy unstructured "experiential learning" approaches such as group exercises and simulations.

You are a highly intuitive thinker who sees patterns and connections that other people do not. You plan before tackling tough problems, and you work hard to ensure that facts, not feelings, inform your decisions. Because abstract conceptualization is a particular strength, you probably find it easy to discern similarities among apparently dissimilar concepts, things, or people. You are comfortable with uncertainty or ambiguity, but your natural insight tends to limit their influence in your learning environment. Your instructors and fellow learners might regard you as a highly integrated thinker.
Personal Learning Styles.Developmental Suggestions: Matt, your responses indicate that you tend to prefer the Activist learning style less than other styles. In order to develop an integrated learning strategy, you should emphasize your critical thinking skills when listening to lectures or presentations. Employ a healthy dose of skepticism even when learning from "experts." Always seek out evidence that supports (or contradicts) the assertions made by others. Contribute to class discussions and ask questions. Volunteer to participate in experiments or demonstrations.
Personal Learning Styles.Sensory Learning Style:
Your responses indicate that you prefer the Tactile Sensory Learning Style. You are probably already skilled at:
Remembering things by writing them down several times.
Conducting experiments that reinforce basic concepts.
Discovering how things work by taking them apart.
In order to develop a more balanced learning style, you may want to try the following:
Creating charts and graphs to make sense of complex information.
Remembering things by picturing them in your head.
Using an outline to follow along during lectures.
Listening attentively to lengthy speeches or lectures.
Understanding and following oral instructions.
Remembering things by repeating them aloud.

Decision Making Style.Intro:
How you make decisions is influenced not only by your work environment and job responsibilities, but also by your individual personality characteristics.
One common approach to decision-making -- the Rational Decision-Making Style -- relies heavily on observing and analyzing concrete facts. Another common approach -- the Intuitive Decision-Making Style -- relies heavily on a person's intangible "gut feelings." Neither approach should be considered consistently superior to the other. Most people employ a combination of both approaches when they make decisions.
Decision Making Style.Description: Tom, your responses indicate that you tend to favor the Rational approach to decision-making. You approach the decision-making process carefully, and with a desire to avoid making mistakes. It is likely that you attempt to make decisions only after considering all the facts.

Leadership Style.Description: You perform your leadership role by accepting whatever comes your way, adjusting to it and then pushing ahead with persistence. You require some time to be alone and to think matters through. You focus on quality results from your people.

Emotional Intelligence.Description: Tom, your responses indicate that your Emotional Intelligence is well developed. Energy Level.Intro:
Energy Level indicates an individual's current degree of vigor, alertness, and responsiveness. Energy Level measures capacity for activity, and Energy can be thought of as an individual's "battery power." Energy might be depleted at an increased rate while operating within a stressful environment or in a managerial role. Food, sleep, and relaxation can recharge one's Energy.

Increased susceptibility to accidents and mental errors.
Reduced ability to concentrate.
Reversion from Public Self (Environmental/Role Adjustment) to Basic Self (the Primary Profile).
Energy Level.Description: Tom, you currently have a High Energy Level, and can function effectively in a demanding environment. This means you can handle longer days and stress-related situations when necessary to accomplish your goals.

Proactivity.Intro: The Proactive Personality Scale measures how likely an individual is to intentionally and actively create positive change in the work environment. High proactive personalities identify opportunities and act on them; they show initiative, take action, and persevere until they bring about meaningful change. They will often seek ways to improve the work environment as necessary to facilitate effective job performance. Individuals lower on the Proactive Personality Scale will exhibit these behavioral tendencies less regularly.
Proactivity.Description:
Tom, your responses indicate that you work hard to develop and improve yourself, other individuals, organizations, and even the world. You have a high achievement-orientation, and you want your accomplishments to have real impact in the lives of your colleagues. You enjoy developing well-thought out plans that will eventually bring about constructive change. You are most comfortable in an organization that shares your deeply held values. When planning, you tend to use a systems approach to ensure that your actions will provide benefits for the greatest number of people.
You may tend to make job changes more frequently than others in order to experience new challenges, responsibilities, or corporate cultures. You learn best by jumping in and trying new things yourself. You rarely hesitate to take the lead and charge ahead - even in the face of uncertainty.

Self-Monitoring.Intro: The Self-Monitoring Scale measures two dimensions of an individual's personality: Behavioral Flexibility and Career Mobility. High Self-Monitors usually find it easy to "flex" their workplace behaviors in order to adapt to specific situations or individuals. High Self-Monitors are comfortable performing a wide variety of roles, and are skilled at impression management and interpersonal communication. High Self-Monitors are more likely to actively seek promotions, and to follow opportunity to different departments, companies or industries. A High Self-Monitor is more likely than others to relocate geographically in pursuit of opportunity. An individual's Self-Monitoring rating does not predict technical ability or performance on job-related tasks.
Self-Monitoring.Description:
Tom, your responses indicate that you value principles over pragmatism. It is important to you that your career is aligned with your personal beliefs. Your behavior at work usually reflects your deeply held values. You value honesty, and you are sometimes surprised by the dishonesty of others.
You prefer to create your own specialized role or niche within an organization. You tend to maintain a firm boundary between your professional life and personal life.
You are not making significant behavioral adjustments in response to the demands of your current environment. This may mean that you do not consider your current environment particularly challenging, or that your current position constitutes a "natural fit" for you.

Personal Learning Styles.Intro: The four main learning styles are Activist, Reflector, Theorist, and Pragmatist. The Activist engages enthusiastically in new experiences. The Reflector prefers quietly gathering and pondering information. The Theorist enjoys abstract concepts and logical analysis. The Pragmatist uses a trial-and-error approach to problem solving, and prefers that learning have practical applications.
Personal Learning Styles.Description:
Your primary learning style preference is: Reflector. It is highly probable that you also employ other learning strategies in addition to your primary style, depending on the educational context. The relative strengths of your learning styles are presented in the graph at the bottom of this section. The following profile summary describes your preferred approach to learning.
Tom, your responses indicate that you are an exceptionally patient observer of people and things. You have an impartial, careful, and contemplative approach to learning that relies heavily on your intuitive perceptions about human nature. You enjoy quietly pondering ideas and concepts, and you always seek fair solutions. Before forming an opinion, you consider all of the information available, you take your time, and you do not like to be rushed.

You tend to prefer learning situations that allow you to be an objective observer. You prefer that lecturers focus primarily on integrative conceptual models, and not on conflict and controversy. During group discussions, it is likely that you express your opinions diplomatically. In the classroom, you probably tend to keep a relatively low profile. You listen closely to what other people say, and you can probably detect meaning in their vocal tone and body language, as well.
Personal Learning Styles.Developmental Suggestions: Tom, your responses indicate that you tend to prefer the Pragmatist learning style less than other styles. In order to develop an integrated learning strategy, you should take advantage of any opportunity to engage in experiments or group discussions. When learning a new idea or concept, think of practical applications for this knowledge. Try to see the opportunities inherent in every problem. Remember that work expands to fill the time allotted for it; make a realistic work schedule and stick to it. When asking questions or participating in group discussions, get straight to the point.
Personal Learning Styles.Sensory Learning Style:
Your responses indicate that you prefer the Visual Sensory Learning Style. You are probably already skilled at:
Creating charts and graphs to make sense of complex information.
Remembering things by picturing them in your head.
Using an outline to follow along during lectures.
In order to develop a more balanced learning style, you may want to try the following:
Listening attentively to lengthy speeches or lectures.
Understanding and following oral instructions.
Remembering things by repeating them aloud.
Remembering things by writing them down several times.
Conducting experiments that reinforce basic concepts.
Discovering how things work by taking them apart.

You show sensitivity and responsiveness when it comes to motivating praise and demotivating criticism. Decision Making Style.Intro:

One common approach to decision-making -- the Rational Decision-Making Style -- relies heavily on observing and analyzing concrete facts. Another common approach -- the Intuitive Decision-Making Style -- relies heavily on a person's intangible "gut feelings." Neither approach should be considered consistently superior to the other. Most people employ a combination of both approaches when they make decisions.

However, since you place only a moderate emphasis on these two common approaches, it is likely that achieving results and reaching goals are your primary concerns when making decisions.

It is likely that you are proficient in both maintaining relationships and networking. You think before speaking, and form carefully considered judgments about people or situations. Furthermore, you are aware of the impact of your emotions on others.

Energy Level.Intro:
Energy Level indicates an individual's current degree of vigor, alertness, and responsiveness. Energy Level measures capacity for activity, and Energy can be thought of as an individual's "battery power." Energy might be depleted at an increased rate while operating within a stressful environment or in a managerial role. Food, sleep, and relaxation can recharge one's Energy.
When energy runs out, the following symptoms tend to appear:
Increased susceptibility to accidents and mental errors.
Reduced ability to concentrate.
Reversion from Public Self (Environmental/Role Adjustment) to Basic Self (the Primary Profile).
Energy Level.Description: Katie, you currently have a Very High Energy Level. You are highly effective in demanding environments. Proactivity.Intro: The Proactive Personality Scale measures how likely an individual is to intentionally and actively create positive change in the work environment. High proactive personalities identify opportunities and act on them; they show initiative, take action, and persevere until they bring about meaningful change. They will often seek ways to improve the work environment as necessary to facilitate effective job performance. Individuals lower on the Proactive Personality Scale will exhibit these behavioral tendencies less regularly.
Proactivity.Description:
Katie, your responses indicate that you are dedicated to changing things, in an intended direction, for the better. By focusing on quality, efficiency, and consistency, you create demand for your organization's products and services.
You are always looking for a better way of doing things. Traditional approaches, even if proven and efficient, do not appeal to you. You enjoy being a pioneer in your field or industry.

Self-Monitoring.Intro: The Self-Monitoring Scale measures two dimensions of an individual's personality: Behavioral Flexibility and Career Mobility. High Self-Monitors usually find it easy to "flex" their workplace behaviors in order to adapt to specific situations or individuals. High Self-Monitors are comfortable performing a wide variety of roles, and are skilled at impression management and interpersonal communication. High Self-Monitors are more likely to actively seek promotions, and to follow opportunity to different departments, companies or industries. A High Self-Monitor is more likely than others to relocate geographically in pursuit of opportunity. An individual's Self-Monitoring rating does not predict technical ability or performance on job-related tasks.
Self-Monitoring.Description:
Katie, your responses indicate that you are committed and principled in your approach to work. You are less responsive than other people to shifting environmental demands. You tend to behave consistently even in stressful situations. It may frustrate you that such consistency is not regularly recognized or rewarded.
You value the freedom to pursue work that is compatible to your own interests. You work best when supervisors provide guidance but refrain from micro-managing.
The behavioral adjustments you are making in response to the demands of your current environment are within the normal range. Any additional demands might begin to negatively impact your effectiveness.

Personal Learning Styles.Intro: The four main learning styles are Activist, Reflector, Theorist, and Pragmatist. The Activist engages enthusiastically in new experiences. The Reflector prefers quietly gathering and pondering information. The Theorist enjoys abstract concepts and logical analysis. The Pragmatist uses a trial-and-error approach to problem solving, and prefers that learning have practical applications.
Personal Learning Styles.Description:
Your primary learning style preference is: Reflector. It is highly probable that you also employ other learning strategies in addition to your primary style, depending on the educational context. The relative strengths of your learning styles are presented in the graph at the bottom of this section. The following profile summary describes your preferred approach to learning.
Katie, your responses indicate that you are an exceptionally disciplined observer of people and things. You have an impartial, careful, and precise approach to learning that relies heavily on your finely tuned perception. You enjoy thinking about ideas and concepts, and about how they relate to what you already know. Before forming an opinion, you thoroughly analyze all of the information available, you take your time, and you proceed in an orderly fashion.

You tend to prefer learning situations that allow you to be an objective observer. You prefer that lecturers not stray too far from the planned agenda. During group discussions, you may sometimes say only what is expected, rather than what you really think. In the classroom, you probably tend to keep a relatively low profile. You listen closely to what other people say, although you may silently form objections if their viewpoint is different from your own.
Personal Learning Styles.Developmental Suggestions: Katie, your responses indicate that you tend to prefer the Activist learning style less than other styles. In order to develop an integrated learning strategy, you should emphasize your critical thinking skills when listening to lectures or presentations. Employ a healthy dose of skepticism even when learning from "experts." Always seek out evidence that supports (or contradicts) the assertions made by others. Contribute to class discussions and ask questions. Volunteer to participate in experiments or demonstrations.
Personal Learning Styles.Sensory Learning Style:
Your responses indicate that you prefer the Auditory Sensory Learning Style. You are probably already skilled at:
Listening attentively to lengthy speeches or lectures.
Understanding and following oral instructions.
Remembering things by repeating them aloud.
In order to develop a more balanced learning style, you may want to try the following:
Creating charts and graphs to make sense of complex information.
Remembering things by picturing them in your head.
Using an outline to follow along during lectures.
Remembering things by writing them down several times.
Conducting experiments that reinforce basic concepts.
Discovering how things work by taking them apart.

Self-Monitoring.Description:
Taylor, your responses indicate that you particularly enjoy social situations in which you feel that you can be yourself. You work best when given broad authority to complete tasks in the most expedient way possible. You prefer to focus on the task at hand, and not on deciphering other people's emotional cues.

Personal Learning Styles.Intro: The four main learning styles are Activist, Reflector, Theorist, and Pragmatist. The Activist engages enthusiastically in new experiences. The Reflector prefers quietly gathering and pondering information. The Theorist enjoys abstract concepts and logical analysis. The Pragmatist uses a trial-and-error approach to problem solving, and prefers that learning have practical applications.
Personal Learning Styles.Description:
Your primary learning style preference is: Pragmatist. It is highly probable that you also employ other learning strategies in addition to your primary style, depending on the educational context. The relative strengths of your learning styles are presented in the graph at the bottom of this section. The following profile summary describes your preferred approach to learning.
Taylor, your responses indicate that you prefer a "hands-on" approach to learning that involves active experimentation. You learn best by engaging in group discussions, homework, or other projects. You tend to dislike passive learning situations such as lectures. Because you learn best by doing, not by watching or listening, it is likely that you thoroughly practice new skills until you have mastered them.

You enjoy testing theories to see if they work in the "real world"; and, you consistently think of better, more practical ways to get things done. You see problems as opportunities, and you frequently solve problems by trial and error. You probably spend less time than others preparing for classes or examinations.

Employ a healthy dose of skepticism even when learning from "experts." Always seek out evidence that supports (or contradicts) the assertions made by others. Contribute to class discussions and ask questions. Volunteer to participate in experiments or demonstrations.

Understanding and following oral instructions.

Using an outline to follow along during lectures.
Remembering things by writing them down several times.
Conducting experiments that reinforce basic concepts.
Discovering how things work by taking them apart.

Most people employ a combination of both approaches when they make decisions.
