Leadership Style.Description: You expertly perform your leadership role by managing your people through systems and procedures, and requiring them to follow your organization's rules. You exercise a conservative and watchful style while making sure that projects are handled correctly. You show respect for colleagues' positions and you expect the same in return. You may desire time alone to think matters through and to plan and design systems and procedures. You may prefer to deal with your subordinates one-on-one. You pace yourself and your people well in order to reach your goals.

Emotional Intelligence.Description: Bernard, your responses indicate that your Emotional Intelligence is well developed. You think before speaking, and form carefully considered judgments about people or situations. You are aware of the impact of your emotions on others, and you are able to attune your own style to others' emotional reactions. You work for reasons beyond money or status.

Criteria1.Title: Personal Insight
Criteria1.Intro: The capacity to accurately recognize and understand own emotions as they occur & to base personal self-confidence on an accurate assessment of own abilities.

Criteria2.Title: Self-Discipline
Criteria2.Intro: The capacity to delay gratification when pursuing goals, to speak carefully, & to control negative impulses.

Criteria3.Title: Drive
Criteria3.Intro: The capacity to pursue goals energetically, to relentlessly seek self-improvement, & to persevere despite obstacles or disappointments.

Criteria4.Title: Interpersonal Insight
Criteria4.Intro: The capacity to understand the emotions of others, to adjust own style to interact well with others, & to empathize with alternative perspectives.

Criteria5.Title: Social Agility
Criteria5.Intro: The capacity to be at ease in almost any social situation, to prevent or resolve conflict, to cultivate rapport & build lasting relationships.

Profile Report.Conscientiousness: You balance solid, goal-oriented achievement and a relaxed approach to life. You may fluctuate between periods of highly motivated, achievement-oriented activity and periods of more easy-going behavior. Alternatively, you may demonstrate a sustained, moderate level of effort toward your goals. While you may not demonstrate an intense desire for achievement for its own sake, you are able to summon a sense of purposefulness and self-discipline when needed. The level of your positive motivation toward achievement may depend on the task or situation.

The Movement of Traits.Description: Bernard, your responses indicate how you have been moving your traits in order to adjust to the environmental pressures of your job. These adjustments are from the recent past, usually from 4 to 8 weeks prior to taking the survey. Research shows that individuals alter their Environmental/Role Adjustments at least every 3 to 4 months. Please see the Role Adjustment Profile for monitoring this trait movement activity.

Adjustment1.Description: This indicates that you are exerting more control, assertiveness and forcefulness than is natural for you. This might be caused by the pressures in your environment.

Adjustment2.Description: This indicates that one of two changes is happening to you. Either there has been some disappointment (or let down) by a person or persons; or it has become necessary for you to spend more time alone figuring things out and doing more detail work than usual.

Adjustment3.Description: This indicates that you are needing to slow down and wait for circumstances to happen; or you are trying to be more casual and easygoing, and not pushing as hard as would be typical of you.

Environmental/Role Adjustments (ERAs).How Co-Workers See You:
Bernard, your survey responses indicate that you are flexing your behaviors in order to perform your job and/or meet the demands of your environment. Because your on-the-job behaviors are not the same as your Primary Profile, your co-workers may view you quite differently than how you view yourself. The following paragraph describes how your co-workers and manager(s) may see you when you are adjusting to the pressures of your job:



Bernard, your co-workers may observe that you are steady, conscientious and mild-mannered. You persistently work toward achieving accuracy, and you show a great deal of interest in issues concerning what you feel is right versus wrong. You are a loyal and unassuming perfectionist who desires security and clear guidelines. Your sensitivity to criticism and caution of the new is qualified by your use of gentle persuasion and firmness when making your position known. You may prefer a few close friends rather than many casual acquaintances.

Stress Level.Intro: Stress is not necessarily all bad; some stress can be healthy. The Stress Level measurement indicates how well you are handling your current environmental demands. The "Good" response indicates that you are coping with your environment effectively, while the other responses indicate varying degrees of difficulty in dealing with it.
Stress Level.Description: Bernard, your responses indicate that during the above-mentioned period, your stress level was Mildly Stressed. This indicates that your environment was of some concern to you, probably because goals were not being achieved quickly enough. It may also indicate that you felt that the rewards gained did not justify the effort expended.

Energy Level.Intro:
Energy Level indicates an individual's current degree of vigor, alertness, and responsiveness. Energy Level measures capacity for activity, and Energy can be thought of as an individual's "battery power." Energy might be depleted at an increased rate while operating within a stressful environment or in a managerial role. Food, sleep, and relaxation can recharge one's Energy.
When energy runs out, the following symptoms tend to appear:
Increased susceptibility to accidents and mental errors.
Reduced ability to concentrate.
Reversion from Public Self (Environmental/Role Adjustment) to Basic Self (the Primary Profile).
Energy Level.Description: Bernard, you currently have a High Energy Level, and can function effectively in a demanding environment. This means you can handle longer days and stress-related situations when necessary to accomplish your goals.

Proactivity.Intro: The Proactive Personality Scale measures how likely an individual is to intentionally and actively create positive change in the work environment. High proactive personalities identify opportunities and act on them; they show initiative, take action, and persevere until they bring about meaningful change. They will often seek ways to improve the work environment as necessary to facilitate effective job performance. Individuals lower on the Proactive Personality Scale will exhibit these behavioral tendencies less regularly.
Proactivity.Description:
Bernard, your responses indicate that you are highly aware of the legal and ethical boundaries concerning business behavior. You ensure that all of your actions are strategically sound, politically appropriate, and tactically feasible. For you to support change, it must be clearly defined and proven constructive. As a manager, you tend to make a great effort to ensure that nothing goes wrong. When faced with unfamiliar circumstances, you tend to rely on proven approaches to see you through.
You tend to act decisively to defend your independence. It is likely that you are less concerned with performance standards imposed by others. You may have a tendency to rebel when others are overly judgmental or critical of your unconventional methods.

Self-Monitoring.Intro: The Self-Monitoring Scale measures two dimensions of an individual's personality: Behavioral Flexibility and Career Mobility. High Self-Monitors usually find it easy to "flex" their workplace behaviors in order to adapt to specific situations or individuals. High Self-Monitors are comfortable performing a wide variety of roles, and are skilled at impression management and interpersonal communication. High Self-Monitors are more likely to actively seek promotions, and to follow opportunity to different departments, companies or industries. A High Self-Monitor is more likely than others to relocate geographically in pursuit of opportunity. An individual's Self-Monitoring rating does not predict technical ability or performance on job-related tasks.
Self-Monitoring.Description:
Bernard, your responses indicate that you are committed and principled in your approach to work. You are less responsive than other people to shifting environmental demands. You tend to behave consistently even in stressful situations. It may frustrate you that such consistency is not regularly recognized or rewarded.
You value the freedom to pursue work that is compatible to your own interests. You work best when supervisors provide guidance but refrain from micro-managing.
The behavioral adjustments you are making in response to the demands of your current environment are within the normal range. Any additional demands might begin to negatively impact your effectiveness.

Personal Learning Styles.Intro: The four main learning styles are Activist, Reflector, Theorist, and Pragmatist. The Activist engages enthusiastically in new experiences. The Reflector prefers quietly gathering and pondering information. The Theorist enjoys abstract concepts and logical analysis. The Pragmatist uses a trial-and-error approach to problem solving, and prefers that learning have practical applications.
Personal Learning Styles.Description:
Your primary learning style preference is: Theorist. It is highly probable that you also employ other learning strategies in addition to your primary style, depending on the educational context. The relative strengths of your learning styles are presented in the graph at the bottom of this section. The following profile summary describes your preferred approach to learning.
Bernard, your responses indicate that you prefer an analytical, conceptual approach to learning. You tend to rely heavily on logical thinking and rational evaluation. You probably tend to be more oriented towards things and symbols, and less towards other people. You learn best in authority-directed, impersonal learning situations that emphasize theory and systematic analysis. You probably do not enjoy unstructured "experiential learning" approaches such as group exercises and simulations.

You are a highly disciplined thinker who sees patterns and connections that other people do not. You take a step-by-step approach to problem solving, and you rely on facts, not feelings, when making decisions. Because abstract conceptualization is a particular strength, you probably find it easy to discern similarities among apparently dissimilar concepts, things, or people. You work hard to minimize uncertainty or ambiguity in your learning environment. Your instructors and fellow learners might regard you as a perfectionist.
Personal Learning Styles.Developmental Suggestions: Bernard, your responses indicate that you tend to prefer the Activist learning style less than other styles. In order to develop an integrated learning strategy, you should emphasize your critical thinking skills when listening to lectures or presentations. Employ a healthy dose of skepticism even when learning from "experts." Always seek out evidence that supports (or contradicts) the assertions made by others. Contribute to class discussions and ask questions. Volunteer to participate in experiments or demonstrations.
Personal Learning Styles.Sensory Learning Style:
Your responses indicate that you prefer the Auditory Sensory Learning Style. You are probably already skilled at:
Listening attentively to lengthy speeches or lectures.
Understanding and following oral instructions.
Remembering things by repeating them aloud.
In order to develop a more balanced learning style, you may want to try the following:
Creating charts and graphs to make sense of complex information.
Remembering things by picturing them in your head.
Using an outline to follow along during lectures.
Remembering things by writing them down several times.
Conducting experiments that reinforce basic concepts.
Discovering how things work by taking them apart.

Able to manage a multitude of details

High Trait.Candidate Description:
Ted, in addition to the qualities above which generate the power behind your work, you are a conscientious and skillful professional, who is dutifully committed to achieving exacting standards while meeting pressure and deadlines. You thrive in an environment that allows you to develop or use systems, which you feel are essential to the smooth operation of a program or project.

You show sensitivity and responsiveness when it comes to motivating praise and demotivating criticism. Because of your strong organizational skills, you can be relied upon to implement company policy and to support colleagues, as long as the principles and actions involved are fair to all concerned.

Since you are able to focus on specific tasks while keeping an overall project goal in mind, you are skilled in setting priorities and like to provide your people with exact direction as to when and how a project is to be done. You may prefer to explore all options and avenues when solving problems or when asked to make changes.
High Trait.Sensitive Areas: An undependable, rapidly changing environment wherein systems are being constantly altered or disregarded; and misdirected criticism, which causes a marked decline in the recipient's attitude and productivity.
High Trait.Potential Reactions: When an issue is dealt with in an improper manner or when criticism is unfair, you may react with intense disapproval; and you may be compelled to counteract by overpowering the opposition with appropriate facts or rules.

Other Traits.Intro: Your other three traits, Dominance, Extroversion, and Patience, and their location in your profile are listed in the Summary section, and they are also portrayed on the profile graph. These three traits have a direct effect on your High Trait and how it is interpreted. You will recall that the High Trait accounts for 50 to 70 percent of an individual's behavior; these other three traits account for the remaining 50 to 30 percent.

Interaction1.Descriptive Words: Good With Detail | Fussy | Sensitive To Judgment | Conservative | Formal | Cautious | Conscientious | Very Loyal | A Perfectionist
Interaction1.Summary Paragraph: The distinction between what is right and wrong is very important to you. You want to know if you are accepted in a group, because you desire the security of belonging. It is possible that you further your goals by taking one step at a time, and by envisioning that everything will turn out alright. In accomplishing your activities, you dutifully follow accepted patterns, rules and systems that you respect and feel are fair. You are not critical in public, unless a rule or principle has been broken. It is likely that you function in a rather traditional fashion.

Interaction2.Summary Paragraph: In your work environment, you prefer clearly defined direction from strong leadership, which is both fair and committed to quality.

Interaction3.Descriptive Words: Patient | Sincere | Stable | Non-judgmental | Cooperative | Unhurried
Interaction3.Summary Paragraph: You convey a calm and consistent style. When you are introduced to new material, you undertake to learn it thoroughly and deliberately. It is possible that you develop a few close friends and tend to keep some distance from others until you feel accepted.

Interaction4.Summary Paragraph: You manage to get matters completed, according to your preferred course of action, by using persistent and subtle persuasion. You endeavor to be fair to people and try to prevent embarrassing situations. You relate friendliness in a quiet and reassuring manner.

The security of basic benefits.

because your Extroversion trait is lower

because your Patience trait is higher

Decision Making Style.Intro:
How you make decisions is influenced not only by your work environment and job responsibilities, but also by your individual personality characteristics.
One common approach to decision-making -- the Rational Decision-Making Style -- relies heavily on observing and analyzing concrete facts. Another common approach -- the Intuitive Decision-Making Style -- relies heavily on a person's intangible "gut feelings." Neither approach should be considered consistently superior to the other. Most people employ a combination of both approaches when they make decisions.

Leadership Style.Intro: The range of Leadership Styles is:Authoritative - Persuasive - Mentor - Traditionalist - Adaptable
Leadership Style.Description: You perform your leadership role by accepting whatever comes your way, by adjusting to it and then pushing ahead with persistence. You regard timing as very important and this is reflected in your expertise in systematic planning and in working toward your goals. You tend to delegate authority and may be disinclined to delegate details. You require time to be alone and think matters through in order to be sure that projects are proceeding smoothly.

Emotional Intelligence.Description: Ann, your responses indicate that your Emotional Intelligence is well developed. You think before speaking, and form carefully considered judgments about people or situations. You are aware of the impact of your emotions on others, and you are able to attune your own style to others' emotional reactions. You work for reasons beyond money or status.

Criteria1.Title: Personal Insight
Criteria1.Intro: The capacity to accurately recognize and understand own emotions as they occur & to base personal self-confidence on an accurate assessment of own abilities.

Criteria2.Title: Self-Discipline
Criteria2.Intro: The capacity to delay gratification when pursuing goals, to speak carefully, & to control negative impulses.

Criteria3.Title: Drive
Criteria3.Intro: The capacity to pursue goals energetically, to relentlessly seek self-improvement, & to persevere despite obstacles or disappointments.

Criteria4.Title: Interpersonal Insight
Criteria4.Intro: The capacity to understand the emotions of others, to adjust own style to interact well with others, & to empathize with alternative perspectives.

Criteria5.Title: Social Agility
Criteria5.Intro: The capacity to be at ease in almost any social situation, to prevent or resolve conflict, to cultivate rapport & build lasting relationships.

Profile Report.Conscientiousness: Your personal style tends to be somewhat easy-going and relaxed. You do your best work on tasks that you enjoy, but you may be able to sustain an adequate degree of effort when it comes to essential tasks. You may or may not be concerned about extremely high levels of achievement. You like to maintain a balance between work and relaxation. You may apply positive motivation for consensus-building work activities, but maintaining focus on bottom-line results may be a challenge for you.

The Movement of Traits.Description: Ann, your responses indicate how you have been moving your traits in order to adjust to the environmental pressures of your job. These adjustments are from the recent past, usually from 4 to 8 weeks prior to taking the survey. Research shows that individuals alter their Environmental/Role Adjustments at least every 3 to 4 months. Please see the Role Adjustment Profile for monitoring this trait movement activity.

Adjustment1.Description: This indicates that you are exerting more control, assertiveness and forcefulness than is natural for you. This might be caused by the pressures in your environment.

Adjustment2.Description: This indicates that one of two changes is happening to you. Either there has been some disappointment (or let down) by a person or persons; or it has become necessary for you to spend more time alone figuring things out and doing more detail work than usual.

Adjustment3.Description: This indicates that events are not happening as quickly as you desire. Perhaps deadlines are not being met, and your sense of urgency is increasing with a need to move at a faster pace.

Adjustment4.Description: This indicates that you feel the need to pay more attention to the system and details, while emphasizing preciseness and accuracy. You may be trying to be more organized and systematic, or more cautious and security-conscious.

Environmental/Role Adjustments (ERAs).How Co-Workers See You:
Ann, your survey responses indicate that you are flexing your behaviors in order to perform your job and/or meet the demands of your environment. Because your on-the-job behaviors are not the same as your Primary Profile, your co-workers may view you quite differently than how you view yourself. The following paragraph describes how your co-workers and manager(s) may see you when you are adjusting to the pressures of your job:



Ann, your co-workers may observe that you are very thorough, precise, steady, cooperative and methodical in your approach to life and work. You exercise caution when it pertains to new people, systems and procedures. Your patient, non-rushed and conscientious style is qualified by your insistence that tasks are completed correctly. You are very loyal to people whom you respect. Criticism that is launched in your direction may produce a sensitive reaction on your part.

Stress Level.Intro: Stress is not necessarily all bad; some stress can be healthy. The Stress Level measurement indicates how well you are handling your current environmental demands. The "Good" response indicates that you are coping with your environment effectively, while the other responses indicate varying degrees of difficulty in dealing with it.
Stress Level.Description: Ann, your responses indicate that during the above-mentioned period, your stress level was Mildly Stressed. This indicates that your environment was of some concern to you, probably because goals were not being achieved quickly enough. It may also indicate that you felt that the rewards gained did not justify the effort expended.

Energy Level.Intro:
Energy Level indicates an individual's current degree of vigor, alertness, and responsiveness. Energy Level measures capacity for activity, and Energy can be thought of as an individual's "battery power." Energy might be depleted at an increased rate while operating within a stressful environment or in a managerial role. Food, sleep, and relaxation can recharge one's Energy.
When energy runs out, the following symptoms tend to appear:
Increased susceptibility to accidents and mental errors.
Reduced ability to concentrate.
Reversion from Public Self (Environmental/Role Adjustment) to Basic Self (the Primary Profile).
Energy Level.Description: Ann, you currently have a Very High Energy Level. You are highly effective in demanding environments. If called upon, you can maintain your effectiveness in the face of longer hours and stress-related situations. You are also able to juggle a multitude of tasks, or can intensely focus on a single task as necessary.

Proactivity.Intro: The Proactive Personality Scale measures how likely an individual is to intentionally and actively create positive change in the work environment. High proactive personalities identify opportunities and act on them; they show initiative, take action, and persevere until they bring about meaningful change. They will often seek ways to improve the work environment as necessary to facilitate effective job performance. Individuals lower on the Proactive Personality Scale will exhibit these behavioral tendencies less regularly.
Proactivity.Description:
Ann, your responses indicate that you are more likely to be involved in activities aimed at improving the lives of other people. Your commitment to helping others may have led you to become a corporate, civic, or community leader. It is very likely that you regularly set effective, change-oriented goals. Efficient scheduling and effective allocation of resources are some of your strongest skills. You fear complacency and continually strive to improve your professional performance.
You have a strong "bias for action." Learning by doing works best for you. It is unlikely than you would abandon an idea before trying to implement it in the real world.

Self-Monitoring.Intro: The Self-Monitoring Scale measures two dimensions of an individual's personality: Behavioral Flexibility and Career Mobility. High Self-Monitors usually find it easy to "flex" their workplace behaviors in order to adapt to specific situations or individuals. High Self-Monitors are comfortable performing a wide variety of roles, and are skilled at impression management and interpersonal communication. High Self-Monitors are more likely to actively seek promotions, and to follow opportunity to different departments, companies or industries. A High Self-Monitor is more likely than others to relocate geographically in pursuit of opportunity. An individual's Self-Monitoring rating does not predict technical ability or performance on job-related tasks.
Self-Monitoring.Description:
Ann, your responses indicate that you value principles over pragmatism. It is important to you that your career is aligned with your personal beliefs. Your behavior at work usually reflects your deeply held values. You value honesty, and you are sometimes surprised by the dishonesty of others.
You value the freedom to pursue work that is compatible to your own interests. You work best when supervisors provide guidance but refrain from micro-managing.
You are currently making significant behavioral adjustments to meet the demands of your environment. It is likely that you consider this situation challenging, and that you may be more effective in an environment more naturally suited to your behavioral style.

Personal Learning Styles.Intro: The four main learning styles are Activist, Reflector, Theorist, and Pragmatist. The Activist engages enthusiastically in new experiences. The Reflector prefers quietly gathering and pondering information. The Theorist enjoys abstract concepts and logical analysis. The Pragmatist uses a trial-and-error approach to problem solving, and prefers that learning have practical applications.
Personal Learning Styles.Description:
Your primary learning style preference is: Reflector. It is highly probable that you also employ other learning strategies in addition to your primary style, depending on the educational context. The relative strengths of your learning styles are presented in the graph at the bottom of this section. The following profile summary describes your preferred approach to learning.
Ann, your responses indicate that you are an exceptionally patient observer of people and things. You have an impartial, careful, and contemplative approach to learning that relies heavily on your intuitive perceptions about human nature. You enjoy quietly pondering ideas and concepts, and you always seek fair solutions. Before forming an opinion, you consider all of the information available, you take your time, and you do not like to be rushed.

You tend to prefer learning situations that allow you to be an objective observer. You prefer that lecturers focus primarily on integrative conceptual models, and not on conflict and controversy. During group discussions, it is likely that you express your opinions diplomatically. In the classroom, you probably tend to keep a relatively low profile. You listen closely to what other people say, and you can probably detect meaning in their vocal tone and body language, as well.
Personal Learning Styles.Developmental Suggestions: Ann, your responses indicate that you tend to prefer the Activist learning style less than other styles. In order to develop an integrated learning strategy, you should emphasize your critical thinking skills when listening to lectures or presentations. Employ a healthy dose of skepticism even when learning from "experts." Always seek out evidence that supports (or contradicts) the assertions made by others. Contribute to class discussions and ask questions. Volunteer to participate in experiments or demonstrations.
Personal Learning Styles.Sensory Learning Style:
Your responses indicate that you prefer the Auditory Sensory Learning Style. You are probably already skilled at:
Listening attentively to lengthy speeches or lectures.
Understanding and following oral instructions.
Remembering things by repeating them aloud.
In order to develop a more balanced learning style, you may want to try the following:
Creating charts and graphs to make sense of complex information.
Remembering things by picturing them in your head.
Using an outline to follow along during lectures.
Remembering things by writing them down several times.
Conducting experiments that reinforce basic concepts.
Discovering how things work by taking them apart.

Profile Report.Profile Summary: Sam, your responses indicate that you are an intensely persuasive and determined person. You delegate details and obtain technical support whenever it is possible. Your inclination is to deal with a project's key elements rather than its specifics. Too much definite structure and regulation affects your level of comfort. Since you are a very likeable person who takes time to listen to people, it is probable that you have many acquaintances and friends.

High Trait.Key Points Attributed to Your High Trait:
Articulate and candid business communicator
Exceptional interpersonal skills
Effectual in persuading others through enthusiasm
Able to see whole picture
Effective in relating to another person's point-of-view
High Trait.Candidate Description:
Sam, in addition to the qualities above which generate the power behind your work, you are an optimistic and flexible professional, who thrives on large scale opportunities, and who commits yourself to developing and promoting the visions of others, through nontraditional means or change if required.

You use your sharp insight into the subtleties of people's personalities, and you apply it to establishing lines of communication. You understand people who express a need for an environment which is not too structured and where they are allowed to exercise some independent action.

You enjoy the challenge of working with people and building team participation. While you are effective in developing and delegating staff skills, you may decide to do a lot of the detail work, which does not particularly interest you, in order to enhance your position.

Success is the vital ingredient in a corporate atmosphere that raises your spirit and motivates you to activate others.
High Trait.Sensitive Areas: Being turned off by someone's explosion of ill will directed squarely at you, especially if it is fired off in front of your colleagues.
High Trait.Potential Reactions: If forced into a conflict, you use your strong communications skills and counterattack with a verbal offensive.

Other Traits.Intro: Your other three traits, Dominance, Patience, and Conformity, and their location in your profile are listed in the Summary section, and they are also portrayed on the profile graph. These three traits have a direct effect on your High Trait and how it is interpreted. You will recall that the High Trait accounts for 50 to 70 percent of an individual's behavior; these other three traits account for the remaining 50 to 30 percent.

Interaction1.Descriptive Words: Persuasive | Eager To Please | Congenial | Very Friendly | Outgoing | Enthusiastic | Empathetic
Interaction1.Summary Paragraph: Your social skills are highly effective, and it follows that you enjoy interacting and talking freely with people. You are skilled in your ability to interpret people's personalities. You endeavor to make a positive first impression.

Interaction2.Descriptive Words: Open-minded | Flexible | Dislike Trivia And Details | Prefer To Be On Own | Uninhibited
Interaction2.Summary Paragraph: You enjoy working with big-picture opportunities. You hold little enthusiasm for conducting follow-up. Because you are quite independent, you prefer a limited amount of control over your activities. You show interest in being liked, if it does not involve you in complicated relationships.

Interaction3.Summary Paragraph: You are self-assured, especially in your ability to coordinate activities involving people. You have a strong impact upon people, and you do not like being scrutinized and questioned.

Interaction4.Descriptive Words: Casual | Warm | Unhurried | Determined | Steady | Easygoing
Interaction4.Summary Paragraph: You take the time to listen to people. You pursue opportunities with persistence, even when setbacks occur, and you do so with a pleasant disposition that avoids offending people.

Motivational Needs.Primary Motivators:
Because your High Trait is Extroversion, you will need some of the following factors in your environment in order to be self-motivated and highly productive:
A lot of interaction with people.
Meeting new people and making friends.
Opportunities to make more money and improve status.
Being a team player within the organization.
Praise and public recognition.
Identifying with an organization that has prestige and a good public image.
Awareness of what is going on in the organization.
Acceptance and being liked by others.
Motivational Needs.Primary Demotivators:
In contrast, you are likely to be demotivated when:
You perceive you are not liked.
You are not invited into meetings with your peers.
Your territory, (opportunity) is reduced in size.
You feel you are not part of the team.
You do not have enough people contact.
Motivational Needs.Other Motivators:
These motivators are based on the other three traits: because your Extroversion trait is higher
Opportunities for people-interaction.
Meeting new people and making friends.
The team approach to getting things done.
Opportunities for success and status.
because your Patience trait is higher
A stable and harmonious working environment.
A minimum of personality conflicts.
Adequate time to adjust.
A limited number of last minute time pressures.
because your Conformity trait is lower
Freedom from rules, details, and reports.
A generous amount of independence and unusual assignments.
New methods of doing things away from tradition.

Decision Making Style.Intro:
How you make decisions is influenced not only by your work environment and job responsibilities, but also by your individual personality characteristics.
One common approach to decision-making -- the Rational Decision-Making Style -- relies heavily on observing and analyzing concrete facts. Another common approach -- the Intuitive Decision-Making Style -- relies heavily on a person's intangible "gut feelings." Neither approach should be considered consistently superior to the other. Most people employ a combination of both approaches when they make decisions.
Decision Making Style.Description: Sam, your responses indicate that you tend to employ a balanced combination of the Rational and Intuitive approaches to decision-making. However, since you place only a moderate emphasis on these two common approaches, it is likely that achieving results and reaching goals are your primary concerns when making decisions.

Leadership Style.Description: You perform your leadership role by using your excellent ability to interpret people's actions and dialogue, and then by persuading them to do things your way. You like a leadership role, function in a manner suitable to your environment, and support teamwork. You develop your people with enthusiasm. You delegate details freely and may be disinclined to delegate authority. You actively promote change and look for new ways of reaching goals.

Emotional Intelligence.Description: Sam, your responses indicate that you tend to pursue your goals energetically, and that you persevere until your goals are achieved. You strive continually for self-improvement, and you are not discouraged by temporary setbacks or disappointments. You work for reasons beyond money or status, and you prefer that your work be aligned with your personal values.

While you may not demonstrate an intense desire for achievement for its own sake, you are able to summon a sense of purposefulness and self-discipline when needed. The level of your positive motivation toward achievement may depend on the task or situation.

Sam, your responses indicate that you are constantly on the lookout for new opportunities. Others are likely to sense that you have a special gift for seeing what is really important, and that you possess a sense of mission. Because of these attributes, people tend to see you as an inspirational leader. Your ease with people and your political savvy help you to distinguish yourself and to add real value to your organization.
You see more clearly than others the transformational possibilities inherent in current circumstances. Because your grand ambitions are supported by your often breakthrough thinking, you feel that you possess the wherewithal to make even the impossible happen. You enjoy spending time alone, contemplating the revision of existing processes, or designing new products or services.

Self-Monitoring.Description:
Sam, your responses indicate that you particularly enjoy social situations in which you feel that you can be yourself. Even in your work environment, you do not mind displaying your personal attitudes and dispositions. You are more likely than most people to be fully committed to your current work relationships. Your internal state often influences your behavior.
You may ignore directives from upper management if these interfere with what you consider to be important tasks. You are less responsive than other people to situational expectations of appropriate behavior.
The behavioral adjustments you are making in response to the demands of your current environment are within the normal range. Any additional demands might begin to negatively impact your effectiveness.

The relative strengths of your learning styles are presented in the graph at the bottom of this section. The following profile summary describes your preferred approach to learning.
Sam, your responses indicate that you are very open to new experiences, especially those that involve meeting new people. Because you approach challenges with energy and enthusiasm, it is possible that you are regarded as someone who relishes the process of learning. You actively seek out new and exciting topics to explore; and, you enjoy classroom discussions and participating in group activities.

You prefer that your learning focus on the experiences of real people. You prefer learning about engaging biographical material, not abstract theories. When you read or hear the opinion of an expert, you are quick to form a counter-opinion. You can be easily bored with routine, or by classes in which student participation is not encouraged. Your judgments may tend to be spontaneous, and strongly influenced by your desire to interact with other people.

In order to develop an integrated learning strategy, you should emphasize your listening skills during lectures or group discussions. Allow some time to elapse before drawing conclusions regarding a new idea or concept. Attend carefully to your scheduling to allow plenty of time for processing new information. During lectures and class discussions, silently paraphrase the ideas of the speaker. See how much you can learn about other people through quiet observation, as opposed to direct inquiry.

Profile Report.Profile Summary: Bill, your responses indicate that you are steady, conscientious and mild-mannered. You persistently work toward achieving accuracy, and you show a great deal of interest in issues concerning what you feel is right versus wrong. You are a loyal and unassuming perfectionist who desires security and clear guidelines. Your sensitivity to criticism and caution of the new is qualified by your use of gentle persuasion and firmness when making your position known. You may prefer a few close friends rather than many casual acquaintances.

High Trait.Key Points Attributed to Your High Trait:
Exceptionally committed, congenial and systematic
Able to manage a multitude of details
Dedicated to maintaining highest quality and efficiency
Willingly assists others to excellence
Supports high ethical standards
High Trait.Candidate Description:
Bill, in addition to the qualities above which generate the power behind your work, you are a conscientious and composed professional, who is dutifully committed to achieving exacting standards while meeting pressure and deadlines. You thrive in a temperate environment that allows you to utilize systems, which you may feel are essential to the smooth operation of a program or project. You may show sensitivity and responsiveness to motivating praise and demotivating criticism.

Because of your strong organizational skills, you can be relied upon to implement company policy and to support colleagues, as long as the principles involved are fair to all concerned.

Since you are capable of focusing on specific tasks while keeping an overall project goal in mind, you are skilled in setting priorities and like to provide your people with exact direction as to when and how a project is to be done. You may prefer to explore all options and avenues when asked to make changes or when solving problems.
High Trait.Sensitive Areas: An undependable, rapidly changing environment wherein systems are being constantly changed or disregarded; and misdirected criticism, which provokes a marked decline in the recipient's attitude and productivity.
High Trait.Potential Reactions: When an issue is dealt with in an improper manner or when criticism is unfair, you may react with moderate disapproval; and you may respond by informing the opposition of proper facts or rules directly related to the issue or criticism.

Other Traits.Intro: Your other three traits, Dominance, Extroversion, and Patience, and their location in your profile are listed in the Summary section, and they are also portrayed on the profile graph. These three traits have a direct effect on your High Trait and how it is interpreted. You will recall that the High Trait accounts for 50 to 70 percent of an individual's behavior; these other three traits account for the remaining 50 to 30 percent.

Interaction1.Descriptive Words: Good With Detail | Fussy | Sensitive To Judgment | Conservative | Formal | Cautious | Conscientious | Very Loyal | A Perfectionist
Interaction1.Summary Paragraph: The distinction between what is right and wrong is very important to you. You want to know if you are accepted in a group, because you desire the security of belonging. It is possible that you further your goals by taking one step at a time, and by envisioning that everything will turn out alright. In accomplishing your activities, you dutifully follow accepted patterns, rules and systems that you respect and feel are fair. You are not critical in public, unless a rule or principle has been broken. It is likely that you function in a rather traditional fashion.

Interaction2.Summary Paragraph: In your work environment, you prefer clearly defined direction from strong leadership, which is both fair and committed to quality.

Interaction3.Descriptive Words: Patient | Sincere | Stable | Non-judgmental | Cooperative | Unhurried
Interaction3.Summary Paragraph: You convey a calm and consistent style. When you are introduced to new material, you undertake to learn it thoroughly and deliberately. It is possible that you develop a few close friends and tend to keep some distance from others until you feel accepted.

Interaction4.Summary Paragraph: You manage to get matters completed, according to your preferred course of action, by using persistent and subtle persuasion. You endeavor to be fair to people and try to prevent embarrassing situations. You relate friendliness in a quiet and reassuring manner.

A structured environment that has few sudden or abrupt changes.
The security of basic benefits.
A worked out system and quality products.
Praise for specific accomplishments.
Standard operating procedures and sound actions.
Fairness in dealings with individuals and groups.

You pace yourself and your people well in order to reach your goals.

You tend to control or redirect any of your negative impulses or moods. You perform your work conscientiously, and delay gratification while pursuing your goals. You perform accurate self-appraisals of your talents and abilities. You are able to attune your own style to the emotional reactions of others. You strive continually for self-improvement. You do not allow negative emotions to interfere with your social interactions.

Criteria1.Title: Personal Insight
Criteria1.Intro: The capacity to accurately recognize and understand own emotions as they occur & to base personal self-confidence on an accurate assessment of own abilities.

Criteria2.Title: Self-Discipline
Criteria2.Intro: The capacity to delay gratification when pursuing goals, to speak carefully, & to control negative impulses.

Criteria3.Title: Drive
Criteria3.Intro: The capacity to pursue goals energetically, to relentlessly seek self-improvement, & to persevere despite obstacles or disappointments.

Criteria4.Title: Interpersonal Insight
Criteria4.Intro: The capacity to understand the emotions of others, to adjust own style to interact well with others, & to empathize with alternative perspectives.

Criteria5.Title: Social Agility
Criteria5.Intro: The capacity to be at ease in almost any social situation, to prevent or resolve conflict, to cultivate rapport & build lasting relationships.

Profile Report.Conscientiousness: You work hard, demonstrate competence and are determined to fulfill your goals. Your efforts are effective, and your methods are usually unassuming and considerate. Your positive motivations, combined with discipline, planning and purposefulness, lead you to fulfill your tasks and obligations with excellence in mind.

The Movement of Traits.Description: Bill, your responses indicate how you have been moving your traits in order to adjust to the environmental pressures of your job. These adjustments are from the recent past, usually from 4 to 8 weeks prior to taking the survey. Research shows that individuals alter their Environmental/Role Adjustments at least every 3 to 4 months. Please see the Role Adjustment Profile for monitoring this trait movement activity.

Adjustment1.Description: This indicates that one of two changes is happening to you. Either there has been some disappointment (or let down) by a person or persons; or it has become necessary for you to spend more time alone figuring things out and doing more detail work than usual.

Environmental/Role Adjustments (ERAs).How Co-Workers See You:
Bill, your survey responses indicate that you are flexing your behaviors in order to perform your job and/or meet the demands of your environment. Because your on-the-job behaviors are not the same as your Primary Profile, your co-workers may view you quite differently than how you view yourself. The following paragraph describes how your co-workers and manager(s) may see you when you are adjusting to the pressures of your job:



Bill, your co-workers may observe that you are very thorough, precise, steady, cooperative and methodical in your approach to life and work. You exercise caution when it pertains to new people, systems and procedures. Your patient, non-rushed and conscientious style is qualified by your insistence that tasks are completed correctly. You are very loyal to people whom you respect. Criticism that is launched in your direction may produce a sensitive reaction on your part.

Stress Level.Intro: Stress is not necessarily all bad; some stress can be healthy. The Stress Level measurement indicates how well you are handling your current environmental demands. The "Good" response indicates that you are coping with your environment effectively, while the other responses indicate varying degrees of difficulty in dealing with it.
Stress Level.Description: Bill, your responses indicate that during the above-mentioned period, your stress level was Mildly Stressed. This indicates that your environment was of some concern to you, probably because goals were not being achieved quickly enough. It may also indicate that you felt that the rewards gained did not justify the effort expended.

Energy Level.Intro:
Energy Level indicates an individual's current degree of vigor, alertness, and responsiveness. Energy Level measures capacity for activity, and Energy can be thought of as an individual's "battery power." Energy might be depleted at an increased rate while operating within a stressful environment or in a managerial role. Food, sleep, and relaxation can recharge one's Energy.
When energy runs out, the following symptoms tend to appear:
Increased susceptibility to accidents and mental errors.
Reduced ability to concentrate.
Reversion from Public Self (Environmental/Role Adjustment) to Basic Self (the Primary Profile).
Energy Level.Description: Bill, you currently have a Very High Energy Level. You are highly effective in demanding environments. If called upon, you can maintain your effectiveness in the face of longer hours and stress-related situations. You are also able to juggle a multitude of tasks, or can intensely focus on a single task as necessary.

Proactivity.Intro: The Proactive Personality Scale measures how likely an individual is to intentionally and actively create positive change in the work environment. High proactive personalities identify opportunities and act on them; they show initiative, take action, and persevere until they bring about meaningful change. They will often seek ways to improve the work environment as necessary to facilitate effective job performance. Individuals lower on the Proactive Personality Scale will exhibit these behavioral tendencies less regularly.
Proactivity.Description:
Bill, your responses indicate that you enjoy working towards the constructive reform of established systems. You operate with your organization's best interests in mind. You do not allow your desire to improve existing systems to detract from routine work that must get done, nor do you overstep the bounds of appropriateness in your pursuit of change. You are more likely to be committed to an organization that values and supports your efforts. You are particularly skilled at appraising situations, identifying problems, and allocating resources to find solutions and drive innovation.
You enjoy trying different things, or doing the same things differently. Because you dread the routine and the ordinary, you are probably a consistent champion of change initiatives. Experimentation that leads to innovation is important to you.

Self-Monitoring.Intro: The Self-Monitoring Scale measures two dimensions of an individual's personality: Behavioral Flexibility and Career Mobility. High Self-Monitors usually find it easy to "flex" their workplace behaviors in order to adapt to specific situations or individuals. High Self-Monitors are comfortable performing a wide variety of roles, and are skilled at impression management and interpersonal communication. High Self-Monitors are more likely to actively seek promotions, and to follow opportunity to different departments, companies or industries. A High Self-Monitor is more likely than others to relocate geographically in pursuit of opportunity. An individual's Self-Monitoring rating does not predict technical ability or performance on job-related tasks.
Self-Monitoring.Description:
Bill, your responses indicate that you are committed and principled in your approach to work. You are less responsive than other people to shifting environmental demands. You tend to behave consistently even in stressful situations. It may frustrate you that such consistency is not regularly recognized or rewarded.
You value the freedom to pursue work that is compatible to your own interests. You work best when supervisors provide guidance but refrain from micro-managing.
You are not making significant behavioral adjustments in response to the demands of your current environment. This may mean that you do not consider your current environment particularly challenging, or that your current position constitutes a "natural fit" for you.

Personal Learning Styles.Intro: The four main learning styles are Activist, Reflector, Theorist, and Pragmatist. The Activist engages enthusiastically in new experiences. The Reflector prefers quietly gathering and pondering information. The Theorist enjoys abstract concepts and logical analysis. The Pragmatist uses a trial-and-error approach to problem solving, and prefers that learning have practical applications.
Personal Learning Styles.Description:
Your primary learning style preference is: Theorist. It is highly probable that you also employ other learning strategies in addition to your primary style, depending on the educational context. The relative strengths of your learning styles are presented in the graph at the bottom of this section. The following profile summary describes your preferred approach to learning.
Bill, your responses indicate that you prefer an analytical, conceptual approach to learning. You tend to rely heavily on logical thinking and rational evaluation. You probably tend to be more oriented towards things and symbols, and less towards other people. You learn best in authority-directed, impersonal learning situations that emphasize theory and systematic analysis. You probably do not enjoy unstructured "experiential learning" approaches such as group exercises and simulations.

You are a highly disciplined thinker who sees patterns and connections that other people do not. You take a step-by-step approach to problem solving, and you rely on facts, not feelings, when making decisions. Because abstract conceptualization is a particular strength, you probably find it easy to discern similarities among apparently dissimilar concepts, things, or people. You work hard to minimize uncertainty or ambiguity in your learning environment. Your instructors and fellow learners might regard you as a perfectionist.
Personal Learning Styles.Developmental Suggestions: Bill, your responses indicate that you tend to prefer the Activist learning style less than other styles. In order to develop an integrated learning strategy, you should emphasize your critical thinking skills when listening to lectures or presentations. Employ a healthy dose of skepticism even when learning from "experts." Always seek out evidence that supports (or contradicts) the assertions made by others. Contribute to class discussions and ask questions. Volunteer to participate in experiments or demonstrations.
Personal Learning Styles.Sensory Learning Style:
Your responses indicate that you prefer the Auditory Sensory Learning Style. You are probably already skilled at:
Listening attentively to lengthy speeches or lectures.
Understanding and following oral instructions.
Remembering things by repeating them aloud.
In order to develop a more balanced learning style, you may want to try the following:
Creating charts and graphs to make sense of complex information.
Remembering things by picturing them in your head.
Using an outline to follow along during lectures.
Remembering things by writing them down several times.
Conducting experiments that reinforce basic concepts.
Discovering how things work by taking them apart.

Profile Report.Profile Summary: Jack, your responses indicate that you are very patient and cooperative in nearly every situation. Your dependable and steady nature guides you to make every move count. Since you are easygoing and possess warm friendliness, you do not like to be involved with conflicts and therefore avoid being placed in a position that might cause you to make an enemy. You prefer to be comfortable and peaceful with people. Criticism is a sensitive matter to you. You tend to be a good listener and prefer one-on-one relationships.

High Trait.Description: Pace And Rate Of Motion Trait
High Trait.Key Points Attributed to Your High Trait:
Exceptionally dependable, cooperative and unassuming
Highly motivated to promote a productive, positive work environment
Able to balance task needs with employee needs
Willing listener
Effective in balancing warmth with professionalism
High Trait.Candidate Description:
Jack, in addition to the qualities above which generate the power behind your work, you are a persistent and congenial professional, who is inclined to be effectual in dealing with routine and in analyzing work flow and communication patterns, in order to maximize the effectiveness of your work team.

You may express your cooperative and peace-loving disposition by assisting in the successful resolution of conflicts, by promoting harmonious relationships within your organization, and by assessing co-workers' needs carefully.

You thrive in goal-oriented environment that encourages you to produce at a measured and steady pace, and that allows you to direct your willing energies toward a fixed number of projects to be completed in realistic time frames.
High Trait.Sensitive Areas: Undue pressure that is applied without justification; and a bombardment of diverse projects that need to be completed at once.

Validation of self-worth.

There is strong, capable leadership in your environment.

because your Extroversion trait is lower

because your Conformity trait is higher
A structured environment.

While you may not demonstrate an intense desire for achievement for its own sake, you are able to summon a sense of purposefulness and self-discipline when needed. The level of your positive motivation toward achievement may depend on the task or situation.

The Movement of Traits.Description: Kip, your responses indicate how you have been moving your traits in order to adjust to the environmental pressures of your job. These adjustments are from the recent past, usually from 4 to 8 weeks prior to taking the survey. Research shows that individuals alter their Environmental/Role Adjustments at least every 3 to 4 months. Please see the Role Adjustment Profile for monitoring this trait movement activity.

Adjustment1.Description: This indicates that you are exerting more control, assertiveness and forcefulness than is natural for you. This might be caused by the pressures in your environment.

Adjustment2.Description: This indicates that you are making an effort to be more friendly, empathetic and sensitive in terms of people. It is possible that you are being more communicative in general, or are working to develop more people skills.

Adjustment3.Description: This indicates that you are needing to slow down and wait for circumstances to happen; or you are trying to be more casual and easygoing, and not pushing as hard as would be typical of you.

Environmental/Role Adjustments (ERAs).How Co-Workers See You:
Kip, your survey responses indicate that you are flexing your behaviors in order to perform your job and/or meet the demands of your environment. Because your on-the-job behaviors are not the same as your Primary Profile, your co-workers may view you quite differently than how you view yourself. The following paragraph describes how your co-workers and manager(s) may see you when you are adjusting to the pressures of your job:



Kip, your co-workers may observe that you have a strong sense of loyalty, duty, and fairness to people. You use a great deal of persuasion when communicating with fellow workers, and you focus on completing activities in the correct and prescribed manner. Rushing through tasks does not appeal to you. Detail work, especially as it relates to helping or working with people, is your express capability. You communicate in a friendly, warm, caring, thoughtful and precise manner.

Stress Level.Intro: Stress is not necessarily all bad; some stress can be healthy. The Stress Level measurement indicates how well you are handling your current environmental demands. The "Good" response indicates that you are coping with your environment effectively, while the other responses indicate varying degrees of difficulty in dealing with it.
Stress Level.Description: Kip, your responses indicate that during the above-mentioned period, your stress level was Mildly Stressed. This indicates that your environment was of some concern to you, probably because goals were not being achieved quickly enough. It may also indicate that you felt that the rewards gained did not justify the effort expended.

Energy Level.Intro:
Energy Level indicates an individual's current degree of vigor, alertness, and responsiveness. Energy Level measures capacity for activity, and Energy can be thought of as an individual's "battery power." Energy might be depleted at an increased rate while operating within a stressful environment or in a managerial role. Food, sleep, and relaxation can recharge one's Energy.
When energy runs out, the following symptoms tend to appear:
Increased susceptibility to accidents and mental errors.
Reduced ability to concentrate.
Reversion from Public Self (Environmental/Role Adjustment) to Basic Self (the Primary Profile).
Energy Level.Description: Kip, you currently have a Very High Energy Level. You are highly effective in demanding environments. If called upon, you can maintain your effectiveness in the face of longer hours and stress-related situations. You are also able to juggle a multitude of tasks, or can intensely focus on a single task as necessary.

Proactivity.Intro: The Proactive Personality Scale measures how likely an individual is to intentionally and actively create positive change in the work environment. High proactive personalities identify opportunities and act on them; they show initiative, take action, and persevere until they bring about meaningful change. They will often seek ways to improve the work environment as necessary to facilitate effective job performance. Individuals lower on the Proactive Personality Scale will exhibit these behavioral tendencies less regularly.
Proactivity.Description:
Kip, your responses indicate that you enjoy working towards the constructive reform of established systems. You operate with your organization's best interests in mind. You do not allow your desire to improve existing systems to detract from routine work that must get done, nor do you overstep the bounds of appropriateness in your pursuit of change. You are more likely to be committed to an organization that values and supports your efforts. You are particularly skilled at appraising situations, identifying problems, and allocating resources to find solutions and drive innovation.
You enjoy trying different things, or doing the same things differently. Because you dread the routine and the ordinary, you are probably a consistent champion of change initiatives. Experimentation that leads to innovation is important to you.

Self-Monitoring.Intro: The Self-Monitoring Scale measures two dimensions of an individual's personality: Behavioral Flexibility and Career Mobility. High Self-Monitors usually find it easy to "flex" their workplace behaviors in order to adapt to specific situations or individuals. High Self-Monitors are comfortable performing a wide variety of roles, and are skilled at impression management and interpersonal communication. High Self-Monitors are more likely to actively seek promotions, and to follow opportunity to different departments, companies or industries. A High Self-Monitor is more likely than others to relocate geographically in pursuit of opportunity. An individual's Self-Monitoring rating does not predict technical ability or performance on job-related tasks.
Self-Monitoring.Description:
Kip, your responses indicate that you are committed and principled in your approach to work. You are less responsive than other people to shifting environmental demands. You tend to behave consistently even in stressful situations. It may frustrate you that such consistency is not regularly recognized or rewarded.
You value the freedom to pursue work that is compatible to your own interests. You work best when supervisors provide guidance but refrain from micro-managing.
The behavioral adjustments you are making in response to the demands of your current environment are within the normal range. Any additional demands might begin to negatively impact your effectiveness.

Personal Learning Styles.Intro: The four main learning styles are Activist, Reflector, Theorist, and Pragmatist. The Activist engages enthusiastically in new experiences. The Reflector prefers quietly gathering and pondering information. The Theorist enjoys abstract concepts and logical analysis. The Pragmatist uses a trial-and-error approach to problem solving, and prefers that learning have practical applications.
Personal Learning Styles.Description:
Your primary learning style preference is: Pragmatist. It is highly probable that you also employ other learning strategies in addition to your primary style, depending on the educational context. The relative strengths of your learning styles are presented in the graph at the bottom of this section. The following profile summary describes your preferred approach to learning.
Kip, your responses indicate that you prefer a "hands-on" approach to learning that includes active experimentation. You learn best by engaging in experiments, homework, or other projects. You tend to dislike passive learning situations such as lectures. Because you learn best by doing, not by watching or listening, it is likely that you thoroughly practice new skills until you have mastered them.

You enjoy testing theories to see if they work in the "real world"; and, you consistently think of better, more practical ways to get things done. You frequently solve problems by trial and error, but you try to limit errors if at all possible. In discussions, you tend to get straight to the point; and, you prefer that discussions have an agenda and a time limit. You probably spend less time than others engaging is social small talk before classes or lectures.
Personal Learning Styles.Developmental Suggestions: Kip, your responses indicate that you tend to prefer the Reflector learning style less than other styles. In order to develop an integrated learning strategy, you should emphasize your listening skills during lectures or group discussions. Allow some time to elapse before drawing conclusions regarding a new idea or concept. Attend carefully to your scheduling to allow plenty of time for processing new information. During lectures and class discussions, silently paraphrase the ideas of the speaker. See how much you can learn about other people through quiet observation, as opposed to direct inquiry.
Personal Learning Styles.Sensory Learning Style:
Your responses indicate that you prefer the Auditory Sensory Learning Style. You are probably already skilled at:
Listening attentively to lengthy speeches or lectures.
Understanding and following oral instructions.
Remembering things by repeating them aloud.
In order to develop a more balanced learning style, you may want to try the following:
Creating charts and graphs to make sense of complex information.
Remembering things by picturing them in your head.
Using an outline to follow along during lectures.
Remembering things by writing them down several times.
Conducting experiments that reinforce basic concepts.
Discovering how things work by taking them apart.

Profile Report.Profile Summary: Lara, your responses indicate that you are very patient and cooperative in nearly every situation. Your dependable and steady nature guides you to make every move count. Since you are easygoing and possess warm friendliness, you do not like to be involved with conflicts and therefore avoid being placed in a position that might cause you to make an enemy. You prefer to be comfortable and peaceful with people. Criticism is a sensitive matter to you. You tend to be a good listener and prefer one-on-one relationships.

High Trait.Description: Pace And Rate Of Motion Trait
High Trait.Key Points Attributed to Your High Trait:
Highly motivated to promote a productive, positive work environment
Perceptive listener
Exceptionally dependable and persistent
Able to balance task needs with employee needs
Effective in balancing warmth with professionalism
High Trait.Candidate Description:
Lara, in addition to the qualities above which generate the power behind your work, you are a collaborative professional, who is inclined to be highly effectual in dealing with routine and in analyzing work flow and communication patterns, in order to maximize the effectiveness of your work team.

You may express your cooperative and supportive disposition by assisting in the successful resolution of conflicts, by promoting harmonious relationships within your organization, and by assessing co-workers' needs carefully.

You thrive in a goal-oriented environment that encourages you to produce at a measured and steady pace, that allows you to direct your energies toward a fixed number of projects to be completed in realistic time frames, and that recognizes your competitive qualities are aimed at realizing success not only for yourself but for your organization.
High Trait.Sensitive Areas: Undue pressure that is applied without justification; and a bombardment of diverse projects that need to be completed at once.
High Trait.Potential Reactions: In order to avoid confrontation, you may appear to accept unfavorable conditions imposed upon you. However, after an appropriate interlude, you will work quietly to restore balance to the situation.

Other Traits.Intro: Your other three traits, Dominance, Extroversion, and Conformity, and their location in your profile are listed in the Summary section, and they are also portrayed on the profile graph. These three traits have a direct effect on your High Trait and how it is interpreted. You will recall that the High Trait accounts for 50 to 70 percent of an individual's behavior; these other three traits account for the remaining 50 to 30 percent.

Interaction1.Descriptive Words: Very Cooperative | Delegate Detail | Roll With The Punches | Avoid Conflict | Thorough | Methodical | Good Memory | Non-judgmental | Easygoing | Casual | Often Artistic
Interaction1.Summary Paragraph: You respond strongly to the condition of your work environment and to the people it contains. You focus on making your every move count and do not waste physical energy. You do not become preoccupied with or delayed by detail work and quality concerns, especially when speed is required. You show skill in attending to activities which require repetitive action.

Interaction2.Summary Paragraph: You function most effectively when you are given guidance and direction, and when you know what is expected and in what time frame. You appreciate strong leadership in your environment.

Interaction3.Descriptive Words: Loyal | Cautious | Conscientious | Sensitive To Criticism | Careful
Interaction3.Summary Paragraph: You like to organize your activities and may make lists in order to facilitate this. You are inclined to be very dedicated to strong leaders whom you respect. You strive to be fair in your dealings with people. You tend to be skeptical of unproven new concepts, until you have had time to prove that they work effectively.

Interaction4.Summary Paragraph: Your warm and friendly nature causes you to be well accepted on a social basis. Since you are usually patient with people, they are likely to consider you an individual who has the makings for a very good friend. You use a subtle selling style to get matters accomplished in the manner that you desire.

Motivational Needs.Primary Motivators:
Because your High Trait is Patience, you will need some of the following factors in your environment in order to be self-motivated and highly productive:
An environment where there is harmony and cooperation.
A minimum of conflicts and a steady, stable structure.
A pace set for you with no sudden or abrupt changes.
Forewarning of changes so that there is sufficient time to adjust.
Encouragement in regard to creativity.
Knowing how your personal efforts contribute to overall goals.
Validation of self-worth.
Motivational Needs.Primary Demotivators:
In contrast, you are likely to be demotivated when:
Constantly pressured at the last minute.
There are too many personality conflicts.
There are too many sudden changes.
Expectations are too high and/or not clear.
Motivational Needs.Other Motivators:
These motivators are based on the other three traits: because your Dominance trait is lower
There is strong, capable leadership in your environment.
Direction as to what is to be done and when.
A predictable environment that affords a significant amount of protection and peace.
because your Extroversion trait is lower
A limited amount of emotional exposure.
Time alone to think creatively and figure things out.
Respected as a person and taken seriously.
Socializing in a circle where people are known one-on-one.
Time to prepare for group presentations.
because your Conformity trait is higher
A structured environment.
A worked out system for getting the job done.
Security, training and quality products.

Decision Making Style.Intro:
How you make decisions is influenced not only by your work environment and job responsibilities, but also by your individual personality characteristics.
One common approach to decision-making -- the Rational Decision-Making Style -- relies heavily on observing and analyzing concrete facts. Another common approach -- the Intuitive Decision-Making Style -- relies heavily on a person's intangible "gut feelings." Neither approach should be considered consistently superior to the other. Most people employ a combination of both approaches when they make decisions.
Decision Making Style.Description: Lara, your responses indicate that you strongly favor the Rational approach to decision-making. You tend to be well prepared in decision-making situations, and to seek precise answers. You balance your self-disciplined, even methodical, approach with prudence and a genuine concern for ethical principles.

Leadership Style.Intro: The range of Leadership Styles is:Authoritative - Persuasive - Mentor - Traditionalist - Adaptable
Leadership Style.Description: You perform your leadership role by accepting whatever comes your way, by adjusting to it and then pushing ahead with persistence. You regard timing as very important and this is reflected in your expertise in systematic planning and in working toward your goals. You tend to delegate authority and may be disinclined to delegate details. You require time to be alone and think matters through in order to be sure that projects are proceeding smoothly.

Emotional Intelligence.Description: Lara, your responses indicate that your Emotional Intelligence is well developed. You think before speaking, and form carefully considered judgments about people or situations. You are aware of the impact of your emotions on others, and you are able to attune your own style to others' emotional reactions. You work for reasons beyond money or status.

Criteria1.Title: Personal Insight
Criteria1.Intro: The capacity to accurately recognize and understand own emotions as they occur & to base personal self-confidence on an accurate assessment of own abilities.

Criteria2.Title: Self-Discipline
Criteria2.Intro: The capacity to delay gratification when pursuing goals, to speak carefully, & to control negative impulses.

Criteria3.Title: Drive
Criteria3.Intro: The capacity to pursue goals energetically, to relentlessly seek self-improvement, & to persevere despite obstacles or disappointments.

Criteria4.Title: Interpersonal Insight
Criteria4.Intro: The capacity to understand the emotions of others, to adjust own style to interact well with others, & to empathize with alternative perspectives.

Criteria5.Title: Social Agility
Criteria5.Intro: The capacity to be at ease in almost any social situation, to prevent or resolve conflict, to cultivate rapport & build lasting relationships.

Lara, your responses indicate that you probably engage in cognitive restructuring by psychologically reframing or reinterpreting work situations. This can be useful and beneficial, as when a crisis is perceived as an opportunity. It can also be detrimental, as when people deny the existence of real problems, or convince themselves of the viability of an untenable strategy. Be aware that a long run of good performance without serious setbacks and a lack of tough feedback from outside sources can engender complacency.
Your actions may provoke unintentional change - often with a positive outcome. Sometimes it may feel as if you expend extra energy to achieve the same results as others. When faced with uncertainty, you usually display the important attributes of flexibility and adaptability.
