Self-Monitoring.Description:
Lara, your responses indicate that you value principles over pragmatism. It is important to you that your career is aligned with your personal beliefs. Your behavior at work usually reflects your deeply held values. You value honesty, and you are sometimes surprised by the dishonesty of others.
You value the freedom to pursue work that is compatible to your own interests. You work best when supervisors provide guidance but refrain from micro-managing.
The behavioral adjustments you are making in response to the demands of your current environment are within the normal range. Any additional demands might begin to negatively impact your effectiveness.

Personal Learning Styles.Intro: The four main learning styles are Activist, Reflector, Theorist, and Pragmatist. The Activist engages enthusiastically in new experiences. The Reflector prefers quietly gathering and pondering information. The Theorist enjoys abstract concepts and logical analysis. The Pragmatist uses a trial-and-error approach to problem solving, and prefers that learning have practical applications.
Personal Learning Styles.Description:
Your primary learning style preference is: Reflector. It is highly probable that you also employ other learning strategies in addition to your primary style, depending on the educational context. The relative strengths of your learning styles are presented in the graph at the bottom of this section. The following profile summary describes your preferred approach to learning.
Lara, your responses indicate that you are an exceptionally patient observer of people and things. You have an impartial, careful, and contemplative approach to learning that relies heavily on your intuitive perceptions about human nature. You enjoy quietly pondering ideas and concepts, and you always seek fair solutions. Before forming an opinion, you consider all of the information available, you take your time, and you do not like to be rushed.

You tend to prefer learning situations that allow you to be an objective observer. You prefer that lecturers focus primarily on integrative conceptual models, and not on conflict and controversy. During group discussions, it is likely that you express your opinions diplomatically. In the classroom, you probably tend to keep a relatively low profile. You listen closely to what other people say, and you can probably detect meaning in their vocal tone and body language, as well.
Personal Learning Styles.Developmental Suggestions: Lara, your responses indicate that you tend to prefer the Activist learning style less than other styles. In order to develop an integrated learning strategy, you should emphasize your critical thinking skills when listening to lectures or presentations. Employ a healthy dose of skepticism even when learning from "experts." Always seek out evidence that supports (or contradicts) the assertions made by others. Contribute to class discussions and ask questions. Volunteer to participate in experiments or demonstrations.

Listening attentively to lengthy speeches or lectures.
Understanding and following oral instructions.
Remembering things by repeating them aloud.

Using an outline to follow along during lectures.

Conducting experiments that reinforce basic concepts.
Discovering how things work by taking them apart.

Profile Report.Profile Summary: Scott, your responses indicate that you are very precise, and you demand conformity to what is generally accepted as right. Your inclination to be a specialist, in your field of endeavor, is advanced by your interest in systems and structure. You come across in a direct manner, and do not hesitate to express skepticism in a candid tone. You tend to be strict when it comes to following rules, and if asked to enforce them, you use an authoritative approach. Criticism may cause you to react sensitively.

High Trait.Key Points Attributed to Your High Trait:
Exceptionally committed, creative, and systematic
Dedicated to maintaining highest quality and efficiency
Able to manage a multitude of details
Motivates others to excellence
Supports high ethical standards
High Trait.Candidate Description:
Scott, in addition to the qualities above which generate the power behind your work, you are a conscientious and trustworthy professional, who is dutifully committed to achieving exacting standards while meeting pressure and deadlines. You thrive in a serious environment that allows you to invent or utilize systems, which you feel are essential to the smooth operation of a program or project. You may show reserved sensitivity and responsiveness to motivating praise and demotivating criticism.

Due to your strong organizational skills, you can be relied upon to implement company policy and to support colleagues, as long as the principles involved are fair to all concerned.

Because you are able to focus on specific tasks while keeping an overall project goal in mind, you are skilled in setting priorities and you like to provide your people with exact direction as to when and how a project is to be done. You may prefer to carefully explore all options and avenues when solving problems or when asked to make unwanted changes.
High Trait.Sensitive Areas: An undependable, rapidly changing environment wherein systems are being constantly changed or disregarded; and misdirected criticism, which provokes a marked decline in the recipient's attitude and productivity.
High Trait.Potential Reactions: When an issue is dealt with in an improper manner or when criticism is unfair, you may react with guarded disapproval; and you may be compelled to relate a carefully selected choice of facts or rules in order to counteract the issue or criticism.

Other Traits.Intro: Your other three traits, Dominance, Extroversion, and Patience, and their location in your profile are listed in the Summary section, and they are also portrayed on the profile graph. These three traits have a direct effect on your High Trait and how it is interpreted. You will recall that the High Trait accounts for 50 to 70 percent of an individual's behavior; these other three traits account for the remaining 50 to 30 percent.

Interaction1.Descriptive Words: Traditional | Conscientious | Cautious | Accurate | Reserved | Conservative | Thorough | A Perfectionist | Precise
Interaction1.Summary Paragraph: You regard rules, procedures and details in a very careful manner. You appreciate systems and structure and like to be trained thoroughly. You respect position and authority and expect the same consideration in return. Although you emphasize quality over quantity, you want activities to move along quickly. In whatever field of endeavor you choose, it is likely that you are a specialist or may focus on becoming one. You are technically oriented and show excellence in activities that require precision. You show a great deal of pride in your work.

Interaction2.Summary Paragraph: At times you can be direct and to-the-point in your communication style, which may come across as unbending to some people. If you are called upon to work alone or independently, and when a system has been worked out, you accept the assignment readily.

Interaction3.Summary Paragraph: You are inclined to be very innovative, within an organizational structure, and you are probably good at designing systems. You have a creative and inventive mind that leans toward orderly and scientific ends. You possess a results-oriented attitude. You would rather tell than persuade in your communication style.

Interaction4.Summary Paragraph: Your strong sense of urgency leads you to show little patience with mediocrity, trivia or wandering conversations. You want everything completed in a timely and absolutely correct fashion.

Because your High Trait is Conformity, you will need some of the following factors in your environment in order to be self-motivated and highly productive:
A structured environment that has few sudden or abrupt changes.
The security of basic benefits.
A worked out system and quality products.
Praise for specific accomplishments.
Standard operating procedures and sound actions.
Fairness in dealings with individuals and groups.
Time to do things right.
Motivational Needs.Primary Demotivators:
In contrast, you are likely to be demotivated when:
You are constantly criticized.
The rules are changed without plenty of notice.
There is a lack of procedures, quality and fairness.
There is not a worked out system.

Adjustment1.Description: This indicates that you are needing to slow down and wait for circumstances to happen; or you are trying to be more casual and easygoing, and not pushing as hard as would be typical of you.

Environmental/Role Adjustments (ERAs).How Co-Workers See You:
Scott, your survey responses indicate that you are flexing your behaviors in order to perform your job and/or meet the demands of your environment. Because your on-the-job behaviors are not the same as your Primary Profile, your co-workers may view you quite differently than how you view yourself. The following paragraph describes how your co-workers and manager(s) may see you when you are adjusting to the pressures of your job:



Scott, your co-workers may observe that you are very precise, persistent, and scholarly. You utilize a systematic approach to problem solving. Since you are concerned with the accuracy of information, you exercise skepticism and caution in regard to matters that have not been proven. Standard rules, organized procedures, and traditional methods are means in which you find comfort and where you can use your good memory and accumulated data. You recognize what is generally accepted as right or wrong and insist that the right course is taken.

Stress Level.Intro: Stress is not necessarily all bad; some stress can be healthy. The Stress Level measurement indicates how well you are handling your current environmental demands. The "Good" response indicates that you are coping with your environment effectively, while the other responses indicate varying degrees of difficulty in dealing with it.
Stress Level.Description: Scott, your responses indicate that during the above-mentioned period, your stress level was Very Stressed. This indicates that you sometimes felt overwhelmed by the challenges in your environment. You may have desired more support from your colleagues, or for more time to get things done.

Energy Level.Intro:
Energy Level indicates an individual's current degree of vigor, alertness, and responsiveness. Energy Level measures capacity for activity, and Energy can be thought of as an individual's "battery power." Energy might be depleted at an increased rate while operating within a stressful environment or in a managerial role. Food, sleep, and relaxation can recharge one's Energy.
When energy runs out, the following symptoms tend to appear:
Increased susceptibility to accidents and mental errors.
Reduced ability to concentrate.
Reversion from Public Self (Environmental/Role Adjustment) to Basic Self (the Primary Profile).
Energy Level.Description: Scott, you currently have a Very High Energy Level. You are highly effective in demanding environments. If called upon, you can maintain your effectiveness in the face of longer hours and stress-related situations. You are also able to juggle a multitude of tasks, or can intensely focus on a single task as necessary.

Proactivity.Intro: The Proactive Personality Scale measures how likely an individual is to intentionally and actively create positive change in the work environment. High proactive personalities identify opportunities and act on them; they show initiative, take action, and persevere until they bring about meaningful change. They will often seek ways to improve the work environment as necessary to facilitate effective job performance. Individuals lower on the Proactive Personality Scale will exhibit these behavioral tendencies less regularly.
Proactivity.Description:
Scott, your responses indicate that you enjoy working towards the constructive reform of established systems. You operate with your organization's best interests in mind. You do not allow your desire to improve existing systems to detract from routine work that must get done, nor do you overstep the bounds of appropriateness in your pursuit of change. You are more likely to be committed to an organization that values and supports your efforts. You are particularly skilled at appraising situations, identifying problems, and allocating resources to find solutions and drive innovation.
You enjoy trying different things, or doing the same things differently. Because you dread the routine and the ordinary, you are probably a consistent champion of change initiatives. Experimentation that leads to innovation is important to you.

Self-Monitoring.Intro: The Self-Monitoring Scale measures two dimensions of an individual's personality: Behavioral Flexibility and Career Mobility. High Self-Monitors usually find it easy to "flex" their workplace behaviors in order to adapt to specific situations or individuals. High Self-Monitors are comfortable performing a wide variety of roles, and are skilled at impression management and interpersonal communication. High Self-Monitors are more likely to actively seek promotions, and to follow opportunity to different departments, companies or industries. A High Self-Monitor is more likely than others to relocate geographically in pursuit of opportunity. An individual's Self-Monitoring rating does not predict technical ability or performance on job-related tasks.
Self-Monitoring.Description:
Scott, your responses indicate that you are committed and principled in your approach to work. You are less responsive than other people to shifting environmental demands. You tend to behave consistently even in stressful situations. It may frustrate you that such consistency is not regularly recognized or rewarded.
You prefer to create your own specialized role or niche within an organization. You tend to maintain a firm boundary between your professional life and personal life.
The behavioral adjustments you are making in response to the demands of your current environment are within the normal range. Any additional demands might begin to negatively impact your effectiveness.

Personal Learning Styles.Intro: The four main learning styles are Activist, Reflector, Theorist, and Pragmatist. The Activist engages enthusiastically in new experiences. The Reflector prefers quietly gathering and pondering information. The Theorist enjoys abstract concepts and logical analysis. The Pragmatist uses a trial-and-error approach to problem solving, and prefers that learning have practical applications.
Personal Learning Styles.Description:
Your primary learning style preference is: Theorist. It is highly probable that you also employ other learning strategies in addition to your primary style, depending on the educational context. The relative strengths of your learning styles are presented in the graph at the bottom of this section. The following profile summary describes your preferred approach to learning.
Scott, your responses indicate that you prefer an analytical, conceptual approach to learning. You tend to rely heavily on logical thinking and rational evaluation. You probably tend to be more oriented towards things and symbols, and less towards other people. You learn best in authority-directed, impersonal learning situations that emphasize theory and systematic analysis. You probably do not enjoy unstructured "experiential learning" approaches such as group exercises and simulations.

You are a highly disciplined thinker who sees patterns and connections that other people do not. You take a step-by-step approach to problem solving, and you rely on facts, not feelings, when making decisions. Because abstract conceptualization is a particular strength, you probably find it easy to discern similarities among apparently dissimilar concepts, things, or people. You work hard to minimize uncertainty or ambiguity in your learning environment. Your instructors and fellow learners might regard you as a perfectionist.
Personal Learning Styles.Developmental Suggestions: Scott, your responses indicate that you tend to prefer the Activist learning style less than other styles. In order to develop an integrated learning strategy, you should emphasize your critical thinking skills when listening to lectures or presentations. Employ a healthy dose of skepticism even when learning from "experts." Always seek out evidence that supports (or contradicts) the assertions made by others. Contribute to class discussions and ask questions. Volunteer to participate in experiments or demonstrations.
Personal Learning Styles.Sensory Learning Style:
Your responses indicate that you prefer the Auditory Sensory Learning Style. You are probably already skilled at:
Listening attentively to lengthy speeches or lectures.
Understanding and following oral instructions.
Remembering things by repeating them aloud.
In order to develop a more balanced learning style, you may want to try the following:
Creating charts and graphs to make sense of complex information.
Remembering things by picturing them in your head.
Using an outline to follow along during lectures.
Remembering things by writing them down several times.
Conducting experiments that reinforce basic concepts.
Discovering how things work by taking them apart.

Profile Report.Profile Summary: Greg, your responses indicate that you are very precise, and you demand conformity to what is generally accepted as right. Your inclination to be a specialist, in your field of endeavor, is advanced by your interest in systems and structure. You come across in a direct manner, and do not hesitate to express skepticism in a candid tone. You tend to be strict when it comes to following rules, and if asked to enforce them, you use an authoritative approach. Criticism may cause you to react sensitively.

High Trait.Key Points Attributed to Your High Trait:
Exceptionally committed, creative, and systematic
Dedicated to maintaining highest quality and efficiency
Able to manage a multitude of details
Motivates others to excellence
Supports high ethical standards
High Trait.Candidate Description:
Greg, in addition to the qualities above which generate the power behind your work, you are a conscientious and trustworthy professional, who is dutifully committed to achieving exacting standards while meeting pressure and deadlines. You thrive in a serious environment that allows you to invent or utilize systems, which you feel are essential to the smooth operation of a program or project. You may show reserved sensitivity and responsiveness to motivating praise and demotivating criticism.

Due to your strong organizational skills, you can be relied upon to implement company policy and to support colleagues, as long as the principles involved are fair to all concerned.

Because you are able to focus on specific tasks while keeping an overall project goal in mind, you are skilled in setting priorities and you like to provide your people with exact direction as to when and how a project is to be done. You may prefer to carefully explore all options and avenues when solving problems or when asked to make unwanted changes.
High Trait.Sensitive Areas: An undependable, rapidly changing environment wherein systems are being constantly changed or disregarded; and misdirected criticism, which provokes a marked decline in the recipient's attitude and productivity.
High Trait.Potential Reactions: When an issue is dealt with in an improper manner or when criticism is unfair, you may react with guarded disapproval; and you may be compelled to relate a carefully selected choice of facts or rules in order to counteract the issue or criticism.

Other Traits.Intro: Your other three traits, Dominance, Extroversion, and Patience, and their location in your profile are listed in the Summary section, and they are also portrayed on the profile graph. These three traits have a direct effect on your High Trait and how it is interpreted. You will recall that the High Trait accounts for 50 to 70 percent of an individual's behavior; these other three traits account for the remaining 50 to 30 percent.

Interaction1.Descriptive Words: Traditional | Conscientious | Cautious | Accurate | Reserved | Conservative | Thorough | A Perfectionist | Precise
Interaction1.Summary Paragraph: You regard rules, procedures and details in a very careful manner. You appreciate systems and structure and like to be trained thoroughly. You respect position and authority and expect the same consideration in return. Although you emphasize quality over quantity, you want activities to move along quickly. In whatever field of endeavor you choose, it is likely that you are a specialist or may focus on becoming one. You are technically oriented and show excellence in activities that require precision. You show a great deal of pride in your work.

Interaction2.Summary Paragraph: At times you can be direct and to-the-point in your communication style, which may come across as unbending to some people. If you are called upon to work alone or independently, and when a system has been worked out, you accept the assignment readily.

Interaction3.Summary Paragraph: You are inclined to be very innovative, within an organizational structure, and you are probably good at designing systems. You have a creative and inventive mind that leans toward orderly and scientific ends. You possess a results-oriented attitude. You would rather tell than persuade in your communication style.

Interaction4.Summary Paragraph: Your strong sense of urgency leads you to show little patience with mediocrity, trivia or wandering conversations. You want everything completed in a timely and absolutely correct fashion.

Motivational Needs.Primary Motivators:
Because your High Trait is Conformity, you will need some of the following factors in your environment in order to be self-motivated and highly productive:
A structured environment that has few sudden or abrupt changes.
The security of basic benefits.
A worked out system and quality products.
Praise for specific accomplishments.
Standard operating procedures and sound actions.
Fairness in dealings with individuals and groups.
Time to do things right.
Motivational Needs.Primary Demotivators:
In contrast, you are likely to be demotivated when:
You are constantly criticized.
The rules are changed without plenty of notice.
There is a lack of procedures, quality and fairness.
There is not a worked out system.
Motivational Needs.Other Motivators:
These motivators are based on the other three traits: because your Dominance trait is higher
Daily challenges.
Results-oriented approach to project.
Direct, to-the-point communications.
because your Extroversion trait is lower
A limited amount of emotional exposure.
Time alone to think creatively and figure things out.
Respected as a person and taken seriously.
Socializing in a circle where people are known one-on-one.
Time to prepare for group presentations.
because your Patience trait is lower
A fast pace with a lot of variety.
Freedom from routine.
New environments in which to work or play.
Assignments that require quick action.

Decision Making Style.Intro:
How you make decisions is influenced not only by your work environment and job responsibilities, but also by your individual personality characteristics.
One common approach to decision-making -- the Rational Decision-Making Style -- relies heavily on observing and analyzing concrete facts. Another common approach -- the Intuitive Decision-Making Style -- relies heavily on a person's intangible "gut feelings." Neither approach should be considered consistently superior to the other. Most people employ a combination of both approaches when they make decisions.
Decision Making Style.Description: Greg, your responses indicate that you tend to employ a balanced combination of the Rational and Intuitive approaches to decision-making. However, since you place only a moderate emphasis on these two common approaches, it is likely that achieving results and reaching goals are your primary concerns when making decisions.

Leadership Style.Description: You expertly perform your leadership role by demanding that your people follow your organization's rules and policies. You exercise a conservative and watchful style while applying yourself to directing systems and procedures. You are very intent upon having projects completed correctly. You may desire time alone to think matters through and to be satisfied that goals are being reached properly. You may prefer to deal with your subordinates one-on-one. You like a lot of variety and demand action.

Emotional Intelligence.Description: Greg, your responses indicate that you tend to feel at ease in almost any social situation. You easily build rapport, and you tend to control or redirect any of your negative impulses or moods. You perform your work conscientiously, and delay gratification while pursuing your goals. You think before speaking, and form carefully considered judgments about people or situations.

Criteria1.Title: Personal Insight

Criteria2.Intro: The capacity to delay gratification when pursuing goals, to speak carefully, & to control negative impulses.

Criteria3.Title: Drive
Criteria3.Intro: The capacity to pursue goals energetically, to relentlessly seek self-improvement, & to persevere despite obstacles or disappointments.

Criteria4.Title: Interpersonal Insight
Criteria4.Intro: The capacity to understand the emotions of others, to adjust own style to interact well with others, & to empathize with alternative perspectives.

Criteria5.Title: Social Agility
Criteria5.Intro: The capacity to be at ease in almost any social situation, to prevent or resolve conflict, to cultivate rapport & build lasting relationships.

Energy Level.Intro:
Energy Level indicates an individual's current degree of vigor, alertness, and responsiveness. Energy Level measures capacity for activity, and Energy can be thought of as an individual's "battery power." Energy might be depleted at an increased rate while operating within a stressful environment or in a managerial role. Food, sleep, and relaxation can recharge one's Energy.

Increased susceptibility to accidents and mental errors.
Reduced ability to concentrate.
Reversion from Public Self (Environmental/Role Adjustment) to Basic Self (the Primary Profile).
Energy Level.Description: Randy, you currently have a Very High Energy Level. You are highly effective in demanding environments. If called upon, you can maintain your effectiveness in the face of longer hours and stress-related situations. Proactivity.Intro: The Proactive Personality Scale measures how likely an individual is to intentionally and actively create positive change in the work environment. High proactive personalities identify opportunities and act on them; they show initiative, take action, and persevere until they bring about meaningful change. They will often seek ways to improve the work environment as necessary to facilitate effective job performance. Individuals lower on the Proactive Personality Scale will exhibit these behavioral tendencies less regularly.
Proactivity.Description:
Randy, your responses indicate that you are dedicated to changing things, in an intended direction, for the better. You are highly skilled at risk-assessment, but you are not afraid of taking chances, given the appropriate benefit incentive. When faced with change, you excel at instituting new rules for the proper conduct of business. You would prefer to shape the competitive landscape, rather than merely react to the moves of others. By focusing on quality, efficiency, and consistency, you create demand for your organization's products and services.
You are always looking for a better way of doing things. Traditional approaches, even if proven and efficient, do not appeal to you. You enjoy being a pioneer in your field or industry.

Self-Monitoring.Intro: The Self-Monitoring Scale measures two dimensions of an individual's personality: Behavioral Flexibility and Career Mobility. High Self-Monitors usually find it easy to "flex" their workplace behaviors in order to adapt to specific situations or individuals. High Self-Monitors are comfortable performing a wide variety of roles, and are skilled at impression management and interpersonal communication. High Self-Monitors are more likely to actively seek promotions, and to follow opportunity to different departments, companies or industries. Self-Monitoring.Description:
Randy, your responses indicate that you are committed and principled in your approach to work. You are less responsive than other people to shifting environmental demands. You tend to behave consistently even in stressful situations. It may frustrate you that such consistency is not regularly recognized or rewarded.
You work best when given broad authority to complete tasks in the most expedient way possible. You prefer to focus on the task at hand, and not on deciphering other people's emotional cues.
The behavioral adjustments you are making in response to the demands of your current environment are within the normal range. Any additional demands might begin to negatively impact your effectiveness.

Personal Learning Styles.Intro: The four main learning styles are Activist, Reflector, Theorist, and Pragmatist. The Activist engages enthusiastically in new experiences. Randy, your responses indicate that you prefer a "hands-on" approach to learning that includes active experimentation. You learn best by engaging in experiments, homework, or other projects. You tend to dislike passive learning situations such as lectures. Because you learn best by doing, not by watching or listening, it is likely that you thoroughly practice new skills until you have mastered them.

You enjoy testing theories to see if they work in the "real world"; and, you consistently think of better, more practical ways to get things done. Contribute to class discussions and ask questions. Volunteer to participate in experiments or demonstrations.

Understanding and following oral instructions.
Remembering things by repeating them aloud.

Profile Report.Profile Summary: Scot, your responses indicate that you are very thorough, precise, steady, cooperative and methodical in your approach to life and work. You exercise caution when it pertains to new people, systems and procedures. Your patient, non-rushed and conscientious style is qualified by your insistence that tasks are completed correctly. You are very loyal to people whom you respect. Criticism that is launched in your direction may produce a sensitive reaction on your part.

High Trait.Candidate Description:
Scot, in addition to the qualities above which generate the power behind your work, you are a conscientious and skillful professional, who is dutifully committed to achieving exacting standards while meeting pressure and deadlines. You thrive in an environment that allows you to develop or use systems, which you feel are essential to the smooth operation of a program or project.

You show sensitivity and responsiveness when it comes to motivating praise and demotivating criticism. Because of your strong organizational skills, you can be relied upon to implement company policy and to support colleagues, as long as the principles and actions involved are fair to all concerned.

Since you are able to focus on specific tasks while keeping an overall project goal in mind, you are skilled in setting priorities and like to provide your people with exact direction as to when and how a project is to be done. You may prefer to explore all options and avenues when solving problems or when asked to make changes.
High Trait.Sensitive Areas: An undependable, rapidly changing environment wherein systems are being constantly altered or disregarded; and misdirected criticism, which causes a marked decline in the recipient's attitude and productivity.
High Trait.Potential Reactions: When an issue is dealt with in an improper manner or when criticism is unfair, you may react with intense disapproval; and you may be compelled to counteract by overpowering the opposition with appropriate facts or rules.

Other Traits.Intro: Your other three traits, Dominance, Extroversion, and Patience, and their location in your profile are listed in the Summary section, and they are also portrayed on the profile graph. These three traits have a direct effect on your High Trait and how it is interpreted. You will recall that the High Trait accounts for 50 to 70 percent of an individual's behavior; these other three traits account for the remaining 50 to 30 percent.

How you make decisions is influenced not only by your work environment and job responsibilities, but also by your individual personality characteristics.
One common approach to decision-making -- the Rational Decision-Making Style -- relies heavily on observing and analyzing concrete facts. Most people employ a combination of both approaches when they make decisions.

You are very loyal to people whom you respect. Criticism that is launched in your direction may produce a sensitive reaction on your part.

Stress Level.Intro: Stress is not necessarily all bad; some stress can be healthy. The Stress Level measurement indicates how well you are handling your current environmental demands. The "Good" response indicates that you are coping with your environment effectively, while the other responses indicate varying degrees of difficulty in dealing with it.
Stress Level.Description: Marvin, your responses indicate that during the above-mentioned period, your stress level was Mildly Stressed. This indicates that your environment was of some concern to you, probably because goals were not being achieved quickly enough. It may also indicate that you felt that the rewards gained did not justify the effort expended.

Energy Level.Intro:
Energy Level indicates an individual's current degree of vigor, alertness, and responsiveness. Energy Level measures capacity for activity, and Energy can be thought of as an individual's "battery power." Energy might be depleted at an increased rate while operating within a stressful environment or in a managerial role. Food, sleep, and relaxation can recharge one's Energy.
When energy runs out, the following symptoms tend to appear:
Increased susceptibility to accidents and mental errors.
Reduced ability to concentrate.
Reversion from Public Self (Environmental/Role Adjustment) to Basic Self (the Primary Profile).
Energy Level.Description: Marvin, you currently have a Very High Energy Level. You are highly effective in demanding environments. If called upon, you can maintain your effectiveness in the face of longer hours and stress-related situations. You are also able to juggle a multitude of tasks, or can intensely focus on a single task as necessary.

Proactivity.Intro: The Proactive Personality Scale measures how likely an individual is to intentionally and actively create positive change in the work environment. High proactive personalities identify opportunities and act on them; they show initiative, take action, and persevere until they bring about meaningful change. They will often seek ways to improve the work environment as necessary to facilitate effective job performance. Individuals lower on the Proactive Personality Scale will exhibit these behavioral tendencies less regularly.
Proactivity.Description:
Marvin, your responses indicate that you are dedicated to changing things, in an intended direction, for the better. You are highly skilled at risk-assessment, but you are not afraid of taking chances, given the appropriate benefit incentive. When faced with change, you excel at instituting new rules for the proper conduct of business. You would prefer to shape the competitive landscape, rather than merely react to the moves of others. By focusing on quality, efficiency, and consistency, you create demand for your organization's products and services.
You are always looking for a better way of doing things. Traditional approaches, even if proven and efficient, do not appeal to you. You enjoy being a pioneer in your field or industry.

Self-Monitoring.Intro: The Self-Monitoring Scale measures two dimensions of an individual's personality: Behavioral Flexibility and Career Mobility. High Self-Monitors usually find it easy to "flex" their workplace behaviors in order to adapt to specific situations or individuals. High Self-Monitors are comfortable performing a wide variety of roles, and are skilled at impression management and interpersonal communication. High Self-Monitors are more likely to actively seek promotions, and to follow opportunity to different departments, companies or industries. A High Self-Monitor is more likely than others to relocate geographically in pursuit of opportunity. An individual's Self-Monitoring rating does not predict technical ability or performance on job-related tasks.
Self-Monitoring.Description:
Marvin, your responses indicate that you are committed and principled in your approach to work. You are less responsive than other people to shifting environmental demands. You tend to behave consistently even in stressful situations. It may frustrate you that such consistency is not regularly recognized or rewarded.
You prefer to create your own specialized role or niche within an organization. You tend to maintain a firm boundary between your professional life and personal life.
The behavioral adjustments you are making in response to the demands of your current environment are within the normal range. Any additional demands might begin to negatively impact your effectiveness.

Personal Learning Styles.Intro: The four main learning styles are Activist, Reflector, Theorist, and Pragmatist. The Activist engages enthusiastically in new experiences. The Reflector prefers quietly gathering and pondering information. The Theorist enjoys abstract concepts and logical analysis. The Pragmatist uses a trial-and-error approach to problem solving, and prefers that learning have practical applications.
Personal Learning Styles.Description:
Your primary learning style preference is: Theorist. It is highly probable that you also employ other learning strategies in addition to your primary style, depending on the educational context. The relative strengths of your learning styles are presented in the graph at the bottom of this section. The following profile summary describes your preferred approach to learning.
Marvin, your responses indicate that you prefer an analytical, conceptual approach to learning. You tend to rely heavily on logical thinking and rational evaluation. You probably tend to be more oriented towards things and symbols, and less towards other people. You learn best in authority-directed, impersonal learning situations that emphasize theory and systematic analysis. You probably do not enjoy unstructured "experiential learning" approaches such as group exercises and simulations.

You are a highly disciplined thinker who sees patterns and connections that other people do not. You take a step-by-step approach to problem solving, and you rely on facts, not feelings, when making decisions. Because abstract conceptualization is a particular strength, you probably find it easy to discern similarities among apparently dissimilar concepts, things, or people. You work hard to minimize uncertainty or ambiguity in your learning environment. Your instructors and fellow learners might regard you as a perfectionist.
Personal Learning Styles.Developmental Suggestions: Marvin, your responses indicate that you tend to prefer the Activist learning style less than other styles. In order to develop an integrated learning strategy, you should emphasize your critical thinking skills when listening to lectures or presentations. Employ a healthy dose of skepticism even when learning from "experts." Always seek out evidence that supports (or contradicts) the assertions made by others. Contribute to class discussions and ask questions. Volunteer to participate in experiments or demonstrations.
Personal Learning Styles.Sensory Learning Style:
Your responses indicate that you prefer the Auditory Sensory Learning Style. You are probably already skilled at:
Listening attentively to lengthy speeches or lectures.
Understanding and following oral instructions.
Remembering things by repeating them aloud.
In order to develop a more balanced learning style, you may want to try the following:
Creating charts and graphs to make sense of complex information.
Remembering things by picturing them in your head.
Using an outline to follow along during lectures.
Remembering things by writing them down several times.
Conducting experiments that reinforce basic concepts.
Discovering how things work by taking them apart.

Profile Report.Profile Summary: Patrick, your responses indicate that you are very thoughtful about what is right and fair for the people with whom you interact. Your communication style is highly persuasive and articulate. You are inclined to show an unselfish concern for the welfare of others which, when recognized, offers you a sense of accomplishment. At times you place ideals before practical considerations. You show strong devotion to the principles in which you believe. A great deal of action and variety in your environment appeals to you.

High Trait.Key Points Attributed to Your High Trait:
Articulate, fast-paced business communicator
Exceptional interpersonal and collaboration skills
Effectual in persuading others through enthusiasm
Highly motivated toward achievement and surpassing prior goals
Able to relate to another person's point-of-view
High Trait.Candidate Description:
Patrick, in addition to the qualities above which generate the power behind your work, you are a self-motivated and action-oriented professional, who thrives on various opportunities involving change and on developing and promoting the visions of others. You are able to think quickly and verbalize spontaneously.

You use your sharp insight into the subtleties of people's personalities, and apply it to motivating your people to move on a project or program with speed.

You enjoy working with people, you are effective in developing staff skills, and your participation in team leadership and efforts tends to inspire and support your co-workers. Your trusting nature shows a great deal of confidence in what your people can accomplish.

Success is the vital ingredient in a corporate atmosphere that drives your spirit and motivates you to activate others.
High Trait.Sensitive Areas: Being turned off by someone's explosion of ill will directed squarely at you, especially if it is fired off in front of your colleagues.
High Trait.Potential Reactions: If forced into a conflict, you use your strong communication skills and battle it out with a swift, verbal offensive.

Other Traits.Intro: Your other three traits, Dominance, Patience, and Conformity, and their location in your profile are listed in the Summary section, and they are also portrayed on the profile graph. These three traits have a direct effect on your High Trait and how it is interpreted. You will recall that the High Trait accounts for 50 to 70 percent of an individual's behavior; these other three traits account for the remaining 50 to 30 percent.

Interaction1.Descriptive Words: Friendly | Persuasive | Trusting | Outgoing | Idealistic | Empathetic | Enthusiastic
Interaction1.Summary Paragraph: Since you are intuitive with people and are inclined to be accomplished in human relations, it follows that you may prefer to work in a business which focuses upon people. You appreciate having organizational structure and rules explained thoroughly.

Interaction2.Summary Paragraph: You are inclined to care for people and to exhibit an unselfish regard for their welfare. You may prefer that other people make final decisions.

Interaction3.Descriptive Words: A Good Trainer | Not Easygoing | A Perfectionist | A Good Teacher | Loyal | Emphasize Quality | Strict | Conscientious
Interaction3.Summary Paragraph: You are skillful with detail and have a strong drive to complete activities correctly, especially as it relates to people. You follow directions very carefully and prefer to perform duties in the way they have proven to work in the past.

Interaction4.Descriptive Words: Like Change | Fluent Communicator | Fast-paced | Quick In Actions
Interaction4.Summary Paragraph: You are highly motivated to be pleasant and have a strong need to communicate. You tend to be action-oriented. Your communication style is articulate and fluent.

Motivational Needs.Primary Motivators:
Because your High Trait is Extroversion, you will need some of the following factors in your environment in order to be self-motivated and highly productive:
A lot of interaction with people.
Meeting new people and making friends.
Opportunities to make more money and improve status.
Being a team player within the organization.
Praise and public recognition.
Identifying with an organization that has prestige and a good public image.
Awareness of what is going on in the organization.
Acceptance and being liked by others.
Motivational Needs.Primary Demotivators:
In contrast, you are likely to be demotivated when:
You perceive you are not liked.
You are not invited into meetings with your peers.
Your territory, (opportunity) is reduced in size.
You feel you are not part of the team.
You do not have enough people contact.
Motivational Needs.Other Motivators:
These motivators are based on the other three traits: because your Dominance trait is lower
There is strong, capable leadership in your environment.
Direction as to what is to be done and when.
A predictable environment that affords a significant amount of protection and peace.
because your Extroversion trait is higher
Opportunities for people-interaction.
Meeting new people and making friends.
The team approach to getting things done.
Opportunities for success and status.
because your Conformity trait is higher
A structured environment.
A worked out system for getting the job done.
Security, training and quality products.

Decision Making Style.Intro:
How you make decisions is influenced not only by your work environment and job responsibilities, but also by your individual personality characteristics.
One common approach to decision-making -- the Rational Decision-Making Style -- relies heavily on observing and analyzing concrete facts. Another common approach -- the Intuitive Decision-Making Style -- relies heavily on a person's intangible "gut feelings." Neither approach should be considered consistently superior to the other. Most people employ a combination of both approaches when they make decisions.
Decision Making Style.Description: Patrick, your responses indicate that you tend to employ a balanced combination of the Rational and Intuitive approaches to decision-making. Since you place equally high emphasis on both of these two common approaches, it is likely that you make decisions with an appropriate degree of self-confidence.

Leadership Style.Intro: The range of Leadership Styles is:Authoritative - Persuasive - Mentor - Traditionalist - Adaptable
Leadership Style.Description: You perform your leadership role by using your excellent ability to interpret people's actions and dialogue, and then by persuading them to do things your way. You may tend to have a lot of projects going at one time, and you deal with this in a systematic way. You like to build team spirit and then rely upon team decisions. You enthusiastically develop your subordinates. You delegate authority and may be disinclined to delegate details.

Emotional Intelligence.Description: Patrick, your responses indicate that your Emotional Intelligence is exceptionally well developed. You tend to recognize your own emotions, moods, and drives as they occur. You perform accurate self-appraisals of your talents and abilities. You are able to attune your own style to the emotional reactions of others. You strive continually for self-improvement, and you prefer that your work be aligned with your personal values. You tend to feel at ease in almost any social situation. You form carefully considered judgments about people or situations.

Criteria1.Title: Personal Insight
Criteria1.Intro: The capacity to accurately recognize and understand own emotions as they occur & to base personal self-confidence on an accurate assessment of own abilities.

Criteria2.Title: Self-Discipline
Criteria2.Intro: The capacity to delay gratification when pursuing goals, to speak carefully, & to control negative impulses.

Criteria3.Title: Drive
Criteria3.Intro: The capacity to pursue goals energetically, to relentlessly seek self-improvement, & to persevere despite obstacles or disappointments.

Criteria4.Title: Interpersonal Insight
Criteria4.Intro: The capacity to understand the emotions of others, to adjust own style to interact well with others, & to empathize with alternative perspectives.

Criteria5.Title: Social Agility
Criteria5.Intro: The capacity to be at ease in almost any social situation, to prevent or resolve conflict, to cultivate rapport & build lasting relationships.

Profile Report.Conscientiousness: You work hard, demonstrate competence and are determined to fulfill your goals. Your efforts are effective, and your methods are usually unassuming and considerate. Your positive motivations, combined with discipline, planning and purposefulness, lead you to fulfill your tasks and obligations with excellence in mind.

The Movement of Traits.Description: Patrick, your responses indicate how you have been moving your traits in order to adjust to the environmental pressures of your job. These adjustments are from the recent past, usually from 4 to 8 weeks prior to taking the survey. Research shows that individuals alter their Environmental/Role Adjustments at least every 3 to 4 months. Please see the Role Adjustment Profile for monitoring this trait movement activity.

Adjustment1.Description: This indicates that you have gone from being a talkative, outgoing and friendly person to being a more introverted, quiet, and reserved type of person. Perhaps you feel the need to be more detached, in order to find time alone to think matters through.

Adjustment2.Description: This indicates that you have gone from being an urgent, action-oriented person to being more patient and easygoing. Possibly, this is due to being forced to wait for activities to happen due to other people. It might also result from a need to do routine matters in a timely fashion.

Patrick, your survey responses indicate that you are flexing your behaviors in order to perform your job and/or meet the demands of your environment. Because your on-the-job behaviors are not the same as your Primary Profile, your co-workers may view you quite differently than how you view yourself. The following paragraph describes how your co-workers and manager(s) may see you when you are adjusting to the pressures of your job:



Patrick, your co-workers may observe that you are very patient, steady, cooperative and dependable. You take pride in your work, show self-confidence in your abilities, and are able to improve the processes of routine operations. If you are called upon to improvise with equipment or think of time saving methods, you are capable of performing these functions. Reliable systems that have proved successful in past experiences are your preference. While you are effective at setting work schedule priorities, you are demotivated when there are too many projects in progress at one time.

Patrick, your responses indicate that you are constantly on the lookout for new opportunities. Because your grand ambitions are supported by your often breakthrough thinking, you feel that you possess the wherewithal to make even the impossible happen. Patrick, your responses indicate a tendency to pursue opportunities even if that means changing employers or geographic locations. You are skilled at impression management, and you appreciate public awards for striking achievements. You are receptive to external information, and you rely on your social networks when making career decisions. You are more flexible than most people about leaving your current network of colleagues and forming new relationships elsewhere. You take control of social situations, and work to keep interactions flowing smoothly.
You are likely to feel relatively unconstrained about pursuing opportunity wherever it may be found. You are more likely than other people to change employers suddenly.
The behavioral adjustments you are making in response to the demands of your current environment are within the normal range. Your responses indicate that you would probably perform well if your environment suddenly became more challenging.

Decisive, contemplative, direct and confident
Assertive self-starter
Generates innovative, creative ideas
Able to see the whole picture
Risk taker
High Trait.Candidate Description:
Steven, in addition to the qualities above which generate the power behind your work, you are a self-motivated and inventive professional, who is able to work independently; you are an analytical and imaginative thinker, who shows strength in recognizing and solving problems; and you thrive in a serious work environment that allows you to be goal-oriented, resourceful, and critical of mediocrity and indecisiveness.

You express your earnest drive to get things done through a venturesome sphere of action, and you may commit yourself to achieving results above and beyond what is expected.

Your strong personality and your selective choice of words may strike others as rather authoritative. You are a keen advocate of less talk and more action. You appreciate candor and positive productivity in your relations with co-workers. If a project should slip off the course that you planned, you implement new decisions to alter the course and to keep the control, which is essential to your disposition.
High Trait.Sensitive Areas: When people attempt to wield control over you or they interfere with your progress.
High Trait.Potential Reactions: When someone forces you into a corner, you do not hesitate to exert your dominance in order to gain control of the situation.

Interaction2.Summary Paragraph: You prefer to make decisions on your own and to deal with activities by yourself. You tend to be detached except with those people whom you find personally acceptable.

You can be very critical and will always strive to be right. You may hesitate to delegate detail or authority, especially authority, if called upon to do so, and then you may delegate only by degrees.

Interaction4.Summary Paragraph: At times you possess a take it or leave it attitude. You are inclined to resist changing your mind unless you prove yourself wrong. When you are required to repeat duties, you try to find a more efficient method of improving upon your performance. It is likely that you enjoy nature.

Daily challenges.
Tangible results.
A position with power and prestige.
Direct answers and candor in communications.
The respect of the leadership.
A generous amount of freedom from controls, constant supervision and details.

Opportunities to be in charge, make decisions and be responsible for the results achieved.

Stress Level.Intro: Stress is not necessarily all bad; some stress can be healthy. The Stress Level measurement indicates how well you are handling your current environmental demands. The "Good" response indicates that you are coping with your environment effectively, while the other responses indicate varying degrees of difficulty in dealing with it.
Stress Level.Description: Steven, your responses indicate that during the above-mentioned period, your stress level was Mildly Stressed. This indicates that your environment was of some concern to you, probably because goals were not being achieved quickly enough. It may also indicate that you felt that the rewards gained did not justify the effort expended.

Energy Level.Intro:
Energy Level indicates an individual's current degree of vigor, alertness, and responsiveness. Energy Level measures capacity for activity, and Energy can be thought of as an individual's "battery power." Energy might be depleted at an increased rate while operating within a stressful environment or in a managerial role. Food, sleep, and relaxation can recharge one's Energy.
When energy runs out, the following symptoms tend to appear:
Increased susceptibility to accidents and mental errors.
Reduced ability to concentrate.
Reversion from Public Self (Environmental/Role Adjustment) to Basic Self (the Primary Profile).
Energy Level.Description: Steven, you currently have a Very High Energy Level. You are highly effective in demanding environments. If called upon, you can maintain your effectiveness in the face of longer hours and stress-related situations. You are also able to juggle a multitude of tasks, or can intensely focus on a single task as necessary.

Proactivity.Intro: The Proactive Personality Scale measures how likely an individual is to intentionally and actively create positive change in the work environment. High proactive personalities identify opportunities and act on them; they show initiative, take action, and persevere until they bring about meaningful change. They will often seek ways to improve the work environment as necessary to facilitate effective job performance. Individuals lower on the Proactive Personality Scale will exhibit these behavioral tendencies less regularly.
Proactivity.Description:
Steven, your responses indicate that you enjoy taking initiative at work. You are not satisfied until you have achieved tangible results. You consistently push yourself to exceed ordinary performance standards, and to have a significant impact on organizations, people, or situations. It is likely that your perseverance often results in transformational change for both you and your organization. Your professional accomplishments are likely to have wide-ranging impact.
You are highly skilled at anticipating and preventing problems. You prefer to have some general guidelines to follow when taking action. Given proper support, you will work hard to find new ways to add value for your organization.

Self-Monitoring.Intro: The Self-Monitoring Scale measures two dimensions of an individual's personality: Behavioral Flexibility and Career Mobility. High Self-Monitors usually find it easy to "flex" their workplace behaviors in order to adapt to specific situations or individuals. High Self-Monitors are comfortable performing a wide variety of roles, and are skilled at impression management and interpersonal communication. High Self-Monitors are more likely to actively seek promotions, and to follow opportunity to different departments, companies or industries. A High Self-Monitor is more likely than others to relocate geographically in pursuit of opportunity. An individual's Self-Monitoring rating does not predict technical ability or performance on job-related tasks.
Self-Monitoring.Description:
Steven, your responses indicate that you insist on being true to yourself despite social expectations. Your behavior is governed by your deeply held personal values. It is likely that you would not compromise your principles for the sake of advancement. You are more likely than most people to resign from an organization because of philosophical differences.
You prefer to create your own specialized role or niche within an organization. You tend to maintain a firm boundary between your professional life and personal life.
You are not making significant behavioral adjustments in response to the demands of your current environment. This may mean that you do not consider your current environment particularly challenging, or that your current position constitutes a "natural fit" for you.

Personal Learning Styles.Intro: The four main learning styles are Activist, Reflector, Theorist, and Pragmatist. The Activist engages enthusiastically in new experiences. The Reflector prefers quietly gathering and pondering information. The Theorist enjoys abstract concepts and logical analysis. The Pragmatist uses a trial-and-error approach to problem solving, and prefers that learning have practical applications.
Personal Learning Styles.Description:
Your primary learning style preference is: Pragmatist. It is highly probable that you also employ other learning strategies in addition to your primary style, depending on the educational context. The relative strengths of your learning styles are presented in the graph at the bottom of this section. The following profile summary describes your preferred approach to learning.
Steven, your responses indicate that you prefer a "hands-on" approach to learning involving active experimentation. You learn best by engaging in homework, projects, or face-to-face conversations with authorities. You tend to dislike passive learning situations such as lectures. Because you learn best by doing, not by watching or listening, it is likely that you thoroughly practice new skills until you have mastered them.

You enjoy testing theories to see if they work in the "real world"; and, you consistently think of better, more practical ways to get things done. You see problems as opportunities, and you frequently solve problems by trial and error. In discussions, you tend to get straight to the point; and, you prefer that discussions have an agenda and a time limit. You probably spend less time than others preparing for classes or examinations.
Personal Learning Styles.Developmental Suggestions: Steven, your responses indicate that you tend to prefer the Activist learning style less than other styles. In order to develop an integrated learning strategy, you should emphasize your critical thinking skills when listening to lectures or presentations. Employ a healthy dose of skepticism even when learning from "experts." Always seek out evidence that supports (or contradicts) the assertions made by others. Contribute to class discussions and ask questions. Volunteer to participate in experiments or demonstrations.
Personal Learning Styles.Sensory Learning Style:
Your responses indicate that you prefer the Visual Sensory Learning Style. You are probably already skilled at:
Creating charts and graphs to make sense of complex information.
Remembering things by picturing them in your head.
Using an outline to follow along during lectures.
In order to develop a more balanced learning style, you may want to try the following:
Listening attentively to lengthy speeches or lectures.
Understanding and following oral instructions.
Remembering things by repeating them aloud.
Remembering things by writing them down several times.
Conducting experiments that reinforce basic concepts.
Discovering how things work by taking them apart.

Profile Report.Profile Summary: Dennis, your responses indicate that you are very patient, dependable and steady. You possess a well-developed sense of self-reliance and the ability to see the fundamental points in an issue. When time or energy is wasted, you respond disapprovingly. Your warm and friendly nature is best served in one-on-one relationships. You use persuasion when influencing people to help you accomplish your goals.

High Trait.Description: Pace And Rate Of Motion Trait
High Trait.Key Points Attributed to Your High Trait:
Exceptionally dependable, cooperative and unassuming
Highly motivated to promote a productive, positive work environment
Able to balance task needs with employee needs
Willing listener
Effective in balancing warmth with professionalism
High Trait.Candidate Description:
Dennis, in addition to the qualities above which generate the power behind your work, you are a persistent and congenial professional, who is inclined to be effectual in dealing with routine and in analyzing work flow and communication patterns, in order to maximize the effectiveness of your work team.

You may express your cooperative and peace-loving disposition by assisting in the successful resolution of conflicts, by promoting harmonious relationships within your organization, and by assessing co-workers' needs carefully.

You thrive in goal-oriented environment that encourages you to produce at a measured and steady pace, and that allows you to direct your willing energies toward a fixed number of projects to be completed in realistic time frames.
High Trait.Sensitive Areas: Undue pressure that is applied without justification; and a bombardment of diverse projects that need to be completed at once.
High Trait.Potential Reactions: In order to avoid confrontation, you may appear to accept unfavorable conditions imposed upon you. However, after gaining the moral support of friends or colleagues, you will work quietly to restore balance to the situation.

Other Traits.Intro: Your other three traits, Dominance, Extroversion, and Conformity, and their location in your profile are listed in the Summary section, and they are also portrayed on the profile graph. These three traits have a direct effect on your High Trait and how it is interpreted. You will recall that the High Trait accounts for 50 to 70 percent of an individual's behavior; these other three traits account for the remaining 50 to 30 percent.

Interaction1.Descriptive Words: Very Cooperative | Delegate Detail | Roll With The Punches | Non-judgmental | Thorough | Methodical | Good Memory | Easygoing | Casual | Avoid Conflict
Interaction1.Summary Paragraph: You accept just about anything that comes your way and try to make the best of it. The quality of your work environment, and the people within it, is essential to your sense of well-being. Since you are able to make every move count, your physical energy is regulated to respond to a steady rate of performance. You do not labor over detail, and you are able to recognize the importance of quantity over quality when speed is important. You excel in activities that require repetitive action. Your attitude is usually easygoing.

Interaction2.Summary Paragraph: You tend to function most effectively with guidance and direction. You want to know what is expected of you, and in what time frame a project needs to be completed.

Interaction3.Summary Paragraph: Your warm and friendly manner causes you to be well-accepted socially. Since you are usually patient, it is perceived by people that you would probably be a very good friend. You use a subtle selling style in order to get activities completed.

Interaction4.Descriptive Words: Open Minded | Flexible | Dislike Trivia And Details | Uninhibited
Interaction4.Summary Paragraph: You like being involved in big-picture opportunities. Your interest in follow-up work and details in general is limited. Since you are inclined to be independent about controls, it follows that you may prefer to be on your own. You are interested in being liked, if it does not cause complicated relationships.

Motivational Needs.Primary Motivators:
Because your High Trait is Patience, you will need some of the following factors in your environment in order to be self-motivated and highly productive:
An environment where there is harmony and cooperation.
A minimum of conflicts and a steady, stable structure.
A pace set for you with no sudden or abrupt changes.
Forewarning of changes so that there is sufficient time to adjust.
Encouragement in regard to creativity.
Knowing how your personal efforts contribute to overall goals.
Validation of self-worth.
Motivational Needs.Primary Demotivators:
In contrast, you are likely to be demotivated when:
Constantly pressured at the last minute.
There are too many personality conflicts.
There are too many sudden changes.
Expectations are too high and/or not clear.
Motivational Needs.Other Motivators:
These motivators are based on the other three traits: because your Extroversion trait is lower
A limited amount of emotional exposure.
Time alone to think creatively and figure things out.
Respected as a person and taken seriously.
Socializing in a circle where people are known one-on-one.
Time to prepare for group presentations.
because your Patience trait is higher
A stable and harmonious working environment.
A minimum of personality conflicts.
Adequate time to adjust.
A limited number of last minute time pressures.
because your Conformity trait is lower
Freedom from rules, details, and reports.
A generous amount of independence and unusual assignments.
New methods of doing things away from tradition.

Decision Making Style.Intro:
How you make decisions is influenced not only by your work environment and job responsibilities, but also by your individual personality characteristics.
One common approach to decision-making -- the Rational Decision-Making Style -- relies heavily on observing and analyzing concrete facts. Another common approach -- the Intuitive Decision-Making Style -- relies heavily on a person's intangible "gut feelings." Neither approach should be considered consistently superior to the other. Most people employ a combination of both approaches when they make decisions.
Decision Making Style.Description: Dennis, your responses indicate that you tend to employ a balanced combination of the Rational and Intuitive approaches to decision-making. However, since you place only a moderate emphasis on these two common approaches, it is likely that achieving results and reaching goals are your primary concerns when making decisions.

Leadership Style.Intro: The range of Leadership Styles is:Authoritative - Persuasive - Mentor - Traditionalist - Adaptable
Leadership Style.Description: You perform your leadership role by accepting whatever comes your way, by adjusting to it and then pushing ahead with persistence. You regard timing as very important and this is reflected in your expertise in planning and then in pursuing your goals. You freely delegate both authority and details. You like to find new and different methods of accomplishing projects. You require some time to be alone to think matters through.

Emotional Intelligence.Description: Dennis, your responses indicate that you tend to understand the emotional makeup of others, and to accurately sense what other people are feeling. In addition, you are able to attune your own style to the emotional reactions of others. You perform your work conscientiously, and you tend to control or redirect any of your negative impulses or moods. You think before speaking, and form carefully considered judgments about people or situations.

Criteria1.Title: Personal Insight
Criteria1.Intro: The capacity to accurately recognize and understand own emotions as they occur & to base personal self-confidence on an accurate assessment of own abilities.

Criteria2.Title: Self-Discipline
Criteria2.Intro: The capacity to delay gratification when pursuing goals, to speak carefully, & to control negative impulses.

Criteria3.Title: Drive
Criteria3.Intro: The capacity to pursue goals energetically, to relentlessly seek self-improvement, & to persevere despite obstacles or disappointments.

Criteria4.Title: Interpersonal Insight
Criteria4.Intro: The capacity to understand the emotions of others, to adjust own style to interact well with others, & to empathize with alternative perspectives.

Criteria5.Title: Social Agility
Criteria5.Intro: The capacity to be at ease in almost any social situation, to prevent or resolve conflict, to cultivate rapport & build lasting relationships.

Profile Report.Conscientiousness: You balance solid, goal-oriented achievement and a relaxed approach to life. You may fluctuate between periods of highly motivated, achievement-oriented activity and periods of more easy-going behavior. Alternatively, you may demonstrate a sustained, moderate level of effort toward your goals. While you may not demonstrate an intense desire for achievement for its own sake, you are able to summon a sense of purposefulness and self-discipline when needed. The level of your positive motivation toward achievement may depend on the task or situation.

The Movement of Traits.Description: Dennis, your responses indicate how you have been moving your traits in order to adjust to the environmental pressures of your job. These adjustments are from the recent past, usually from 4 to 8 weeks prior to taking the survey. Research shows that individuals alter their Environmental/Role Adjustments at least every 3 to 4 months. Please see the Role Adjustment Profile for monitoring this trait movement activity.

Adjustment1.Description: This indicates that you are exerting more control, assertiveness and forcefulness than is natural for you. This might be caused by the pressures in your environment.

Adjustment2.Description: This indicates that events are not happening as quickly as you desire. Perhaps deadlines are not being met, and your sense of urgency is increasing with a need to move at a faster pace.

Environmental/Role Adjustments (ERAs).How Co-Workers See You:
Dennis, your survey responses indicate that you are flexing your behaviors in order to perform your job and/or meet the demands of your environment. Because your on-the-job behaviors are not the same as your Primary Profile, your co-workers may view you quite differently than how you view yourself. The following paragraph describes how your co-workers and manager(s) may see you when you are adjusting to the pressures of your job:



Dennis, your co-workers may observe that you are very independent. You relate to the main elements of a concept rather than to its particular details. You continuously look for new methods to accomplish projects and remain enthusiastic as long as you see results. Before you act upon a situation, you take the time to listen to those with input and then you evaluate it. Because you are open to people, they see you as especially warm and friendly.

Energy Level.Description: Dennis, you currently have an Above Average Energy Level, and can maintain your effectiveness in the face of longer hours and heavier than normal workloads, when required to do so for short periods of time. Your effectiveness can be diminished when trying to manage a multitude of tasks or projects for an extended period of time.

Individuals lower on the Proactive Personality Scale will exhibit these behavioral tendencies less regularly.
Proactivity.Description:
Dennis, your responses indicate that you are more likely to be involved in activities aimed at improving the lives of other people. Your commitment to helping others may have led you to become a corporate, civic, or community leader. It is very likely that you regularly set effective, change-oriented goals. Efficient scheduling and effective allocation of resources are some of your strongest skills. You fear complacency and continually strive to improve your professional performance.
You have a strong "bias for action." Learning by doing works best for you. It is unlikely than you would abandon an idea before trying to implement it in the real world.
